title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Algal Blooms: Definition and Nature



Algal Blooms is a rapid increase in the population density of algae, particularly phytoplankton, in aquatic environments.
This can be found in both marine and freshwater ecosystems.
It is often characterized by visible accumulation on the water surface, changing coloration to green, brown, or reddish.





Green tide



Not all blooms are harmful; many are natural components of the ecosystem.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The Ecological Role of Blooms

Primary production: Serve as the base of aquatic food webs by converting inorganic nutrients into organic matter.
Nutrient cycling: Contribute significantly to the cycling of nitrogen and phosphorus in aquatic environments.
Oxygen production: As photosynthetic organisms, they contribute to dissolved oxygen levels (though excessive blooms can lead to the opposite effect during decomposition).
Imbalance: Problems arise when blooms become excessive or prolonged, leading to habitat degradation and ecological shifts.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB)



A Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) is a specific type of bloom that poses risks to human health, animal health, or the environment.
Unlike non-harmful blooms, HABs are often dominated by species that produce potent toxins.
These toxins can contaminate drinking water supplies, shellfish beds, and recreational waters.
Risks to public health occur through ingestion, inhalation, or skin contact.





Alexandrium

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Consequences of HABs
Health & economic impacts:

Human & animal health: Exposure can cause gastrointestinal illness, neurological disorders, respiratory problems, and even death.
Economic losses: Impacts industries such as fisheries, aquaculture, tourism, and recreational activities through beach closures and harvest restrictions.
Ecological disruption: Leads to fish kills, loss of biodiversity, and habitat degradation due to hypoxia or direct toxicity.







Public health risk


HAB toxins can contaminate drinking water and seafood, posing risks through ingestion, inhalation, or skin contact.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms


Nutrient pollution (eutrophication):

Primary driver: Excess nitrogen and phosphorus.
Sources: Agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, wastewater discharges, and atmospheric deposition.
Leads to rapid proliferation of phytoplankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms…
Temperature and climate change:

Algal metabolism is temperature-dependent; warmer waters generally promote faster growth and higher biomass.
Seasonal blooms are often triggered by spring warming.
Rising global temperatures are increasing the frequency, intensity, and geographic range of blooms.
Warmer surface waters create a stable layer that traps nutrients and keeps algae in the sunlit euphotic zone.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms…
Light availability:

Phytoplankton require sunlight for photosynthesis; light availability directly influences the growth and distribution of algal populations.
Factors such as water clarity, turbidity, depth of the euphotic zone, and seasonal day length affect the extent of bloom development.
Euphotic zone: The depth at which light penetrates sufficiently for photosynthesis.
In some cases, dense surface blooms can create a self-shading effect, limiting light for organisms deeper in the water column.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms…
Hydrodynamic conditions:

Water movement and circulation patterns play a critical role in the formation and dispersal of blooms.
Calm, stagnant conditions reduce vertical mixing, allowing buoyant species (like some cyanobacteria) to accumulate at the surface.
Physical forces such as currents, tides, and winds can concentrate algal cells in specific areas like bays or inlets.
Conversely, high turbulence and strong currents can disperse blooms, preventing the accumulation of high biomass.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms…
Salinity:

Salinity levels influence the composition and distribution of algal communities.
Species are often adapted to specific salinity ranges; shifts can favor certain algae over others.
In areas where freshwater and seawater mix, fluctuations due to tides and river flow create niches for specific bloom-forming species.
Some harmful species thrive specifically in brackish water conditions during periods of high freshwater runoff.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Algal Blooms…
Biological factors (grazing pressure):

Grazing by zooplankton and other herbivores acts as a natural top-down control on algal populations.
If zooplankton populations decline (due to pollution or increased predation by fish), the reduced grazing pressure allows phytoplankton to proliferate unchecked.
Some harmful algae are unpalatable or toxic to grazers, further reducing grazing rates and facilitating bloom formation.
The balance between nutrient-driven growth (bottom-up) and grazing (top-down) determines whether a bloom occurs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Consequences of Algal Blooms
Ecological impacts



Shading: Dense blooms block sunlight, killing submerged aquatic vegetation (e.g., seagrasses).
Hypoxia: Decomposition of dead algae by bacteria consumes dissolved oxygen, leading to “dead zones.”
Food Web disruption: Altered plankton communities can lead to starvation of higher trophic levels.
Mortality: Direct toxicity or anoxia causes massive fish kills and declines in biodiversity.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Consequences of Algal Blooms…
Human health risks:

Exposure to HAB toxins occurs via ingestion (contaminated seafood/water), inhalation (aerosolized toxins), or skin contact.
Common symptoms include:

Skin rashes and irritation.
Gastrointestinal distress (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea).
Neurological symptoms (muscle cramps, twitching, numbness).
Severe cases: Paralysis, cardiac or respiratory failure.








Caution



Prolonged or repeated exposure to certain algal toxins can lead to more severe conditions, including liver damage and chronic neurological disorders.
Severe exposure can result in the death of humans, wildlife, and domestic pets.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Consequences of Algal Blooms…
Economic losses:



Industry impacts: Significant losses in fisheries, aquaculture, and tourism.
Operational costs: Increased expenses for water treatment and desalination.
Closures: Beach closures and shellfish harvesting bans directly impact coastal livelihoods.
Property values: Decline in waterfront property values due to odors and unsightly scums.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Management Strategies for Algal Blooms
Nutrient management:

Addressing nutrient pollution is crucial for preventing and mitigating algal blooms.
Agricultural BMPs: Implementing best management practices to reduce fertilizer runoff.
Infrastructure: Upgrading wastewater treatment facilities to remove nitrogen and phosphorus.
Restoration: Restoring riparian buffers and wetlands to intercept and filter nutrients before they reach water bodies.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Management Strategies…
Monitoring and Early Warning Systems:

Regular monitoring of water quality parameters, algal biomass, and toxin levels can help detect and predict the onset of algal blooms.
Early warning systems can alert authorities and stakeholders to potential health risks and enable timely management actions.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Management Strategies…
Mechanical and Chemical Control:

Mechanical methods: Aeration, dredging, and physical harvesting of algal biomass can be used to reduce bloom density.
Chemical treatments: Algaecides (e.g., copper sulfate) or flocculants (e.g., clay) can be employed to kill or sink algal cells.







Caution


These methods can have unintended environmental implications, such as releasing toxins from lysed cells or harming non-target species, and should be used with caution.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Management Strategies…
Integrated watershed management:

Adopting an integrated watershed management approach that addresses land-based sources of nutrient pollution, promotes sustainable land use practices, and protects natural ecosystems can help reduce the risk of algal blooms and enhance the resilience of aquatic systems to environmental stressors.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Organisms/Species involved

Nearly 1/4 of these are known to produce toxins.
Causative agents: Dinoflagellates, diatoms, haptophytes, cyanobacteria, and some silicoflagellates.
Terminology: Often referred to as phytoplankton blooms, toxic algae, red tides, or HABs.
Appearance: Streaks of reddish-brown to greenish-yellow floating debris, often extending for several miles.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Different Blooms
Harmless water discolorations
Example species: Gonyaulax poligramma (found in temperate to tropical waters worldwide). * Nature: These are non-toxin producing species. * Indirect harm: While not toxic, massive fish and invertebrate kills may still result under exceptional conditions. * Mechanism: High biomass leads to anoxia (oxygen depletion), as well as sulfide and ammonia accumulation during cell decomposition.





Gonyaulax poligramma

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Different blooms…
Harmless water discolorations

Cyanobacteria: Possess algal characteristics such as chlorophyll and oxygenic photosynthesis.
Habitat: Found across fresh, estuarine, and marine waters.
Toxicity: Freshwater blooms may consist of toxic species, non-toxic species, or a mix of both.
Cyanotoxins:

Produced intracellularly within the cyanobacteria.
Released into the water (extracellularly) primarily through cell death and lysis, rather than active excretion.

Major toxin producers include Microcystis, Anabaena, and Planktothrix.
Impact: Can contaminate drinking water and cause severe health issues in humans and livestock.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Different blooms…

Example of species is Karenia brevis (producing brevetoxins) or Alexandrium spp.
The affected water turns murky, varying in color: purple, pink, red, or green.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview
Dinoflagellates employ two reproductive strategies that enable both rapid population growth and long-term survival through environmental challenges.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Asexual reproduction (primary)

Asexual reproduction is the most common reproductive mode in dinoflagellates and occurs through binary fission.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in asexual reproduction

The cell divides longitudinally along the sulcus (a groove on the cell surface)
Chromosomes remain condensed throughout the cell cycle, which is unusual among eukaryotes
Cells remain haploid (n) during this phase
The protective theca (covering) may be shed before division or split between daughter cells
This allows rapid population growth under favorable conditions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Sexual reproduction – stress response)

Sexual reproduction is triggered by environmental stress and serves as a mechanism for dormancy and genetic recombination.
It is mainly induced by factors such as nutrient depletion and temperature changes.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Sexual reproduction process

Most vegetative cells are haploid
Two compatible haploid gametes fuse to form a diploid zygote (2n)
This creates a Planozygote, which:

Consumes excess fat and oil reserves
Increases in size
Prepares for dormancy

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Resting cysts and dormancy
Under unfavorable conditions, dinoflagellates form protective dormant stages for survival.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The Hypnozygote stage

A hard outer shell forms around the Planozygote
Similar to biological hibernation
Protects the organism during extended periods of environmental stress

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Recovery stage
When favorable conditions return:

The shell of the Hypnozygote breaks
The dinoflagellate enters a temporary Planomeiocyte stage
Regains its normal size and shape
Resumes active vegetative existence

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Dinoflagellates reproduction…






Note


This flexible reproductive strategy makes dinoflagellates highly adaptable organisms capable of thriving in variable marine environments through rapid reproduction during favorable periods and dormancy during environmental stress.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Diatoms life cycle

Diatoms have a complex life cycle that includes both asexual and sexual reproduction.
It has a unique pattern of size reduction through asexual reproduction followed by size restoration through sexual reproduction.
This cycle is driven by their rigid silica frustule structure, which constrains cell division and creates a progressive size reduction problem.
The reduction is periodically corrected through sexual reproduction.
Step in diatoms life cycle:

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
1. Asexual reproduction (dominant phase)
Binary fission:

The diatom nucleus divides mitotically.
The two halves of the frustule (epitheca and hypotheca) separate.
Each daughter cell inherits one parent valve:

One receives the larger epitheca (becomes the new epitheca)
One receives the smaller hypotheca (becomes the new epitheca)

Each daughter regenerates a new, smaller hypotheca inside the inherited valve.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Consequence - progressive size reduction

With each division, the daughter cell that inherited the hypotheca becomes smaller.
Over multiple generations (10-20+ divisions), average population size decreases.
Pennate diatoms: can shrink to ~30% of original size.
Centric diatoms: can shrink to ~50% of original size.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Physiological impact
The size reduction has significant physiological consequences for diatoms:

Smaller cells have reduced metabolic capacity.
Decreased nutrient storage.
Lower reproductive success.
Eventually triggers sexual reproduction.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
2. Sexual reproduction (size restoration)

Occurs when cells reach a species-specific critical minimum size (~30-50% of maximum).
Cells become sexually competent at this threshold.
Sexual reproduction in diatoms also is triggered by some environmental factors such as light, temperature & nutrient availability

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental factors triggering sexual reproduction

Photoperiod changes (day length)
Many diatoms use light as a seasonal cue.

A shift toward shorter days (autumn) often signals the end of the bloom season, triggering sexual reproduction to produce hardy auxospores or resting stages.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental factors…

Temperature shifts

Sudden changes in water temperature (e.g., the cooling of surface waters) can synchronize the sexual cycle across a population, ensuring that many individuals produce gametes simultaneously to maximize fertilization success.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental factors…

Nutrient limitation – When essential nutrients like Nitrogen or Phosphorus become scarce, vegetative growth slows down. Specifically, Silica limitation prevents the formation of new rigid valves during asexual division, forcing the cell to bypass normal division and initiate the auxospore (expansion) phase to survive.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Sexual reproduction by diatom groups
Pennate diatoms

Cells produce gametes.
Most pennates are heterothallic (require two compatible mating types).
Two types of gametes may be involved;

Isogamy: Two similar, motile, non-flagellated gametes fuse (e.g., Navicula).
Anisogamy: One larger non-motile gamete + one smaller motile gamete (eg. Cocconeis).

Gametes fuse to form a zygote, which develops into an auxospore.
An auxospore is a specialized cell that expands to restore the maximum size of the species, allowing the life cycle to continue.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Sexual reproduction by diatom groups
Centric diatoms

In centric diatoms, sexual reproduction is by oogamy which is the union of unlike cells, usually a large non-motile female gamete and a small flagellated motile male gamete.

The Oogamy is the advanced sexual reproduction in which the:

Female gametangium produces large, non-motile egg.
Male gametangium produces small, flagellated sperm.
Sperm swims to fertilize egg and form a zygote.
Zygote expands to form an auxospore, which resets the cell to maximum size.
Examples: Thalassiosira, Melosira

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Auxospore formation

Fertilization produces a zygote.
Zygote expands dramatically, breaking free of size constraints.
Forms an auxospore - a specialized expansion cell lacking rigid frustule.
Auxospore grows to species’s maximum size (size restoration).
New silica valves form around the expanded cell.
Produces an initial cell with full-size frustule.
Asexual reproduction resumes.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
3. Resting stages (survival strategy)

Some diatoms form resting spores (thick-walled, sink to sediments)
Resting spores is formed under environmental stress:

Nutrient depletion
Temperature extremes
Unfavorable light conditions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of resting spores

Thick, heavily silicified walls
Sink to sediments
Metabolically dormant
Can survive months to years
Common in centric diatoms (Chaetoceros, Thalassiosira)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Germination of a resting spore

Occurs when favorable conditions return.
Spring blooms often result from sediment spore germination.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Life cycle summary
Initial Cell (maximum size)
    ↓
Binary Fission (asexual) → Size Reduction
    ↓ (repeated divisions)
Smaller Cells → Physiological Stress
    ↓ (critical size reached)
Sexual Reproduction Triggered
    ↓
Gamete Formation → Fertilization
    ↓
Auxospore (expansion phase)
    ↓
Initial Cell (restored to maximum size)
    ↓
Cycle Repeats

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Diagram of diatom life cycle

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors triggering diatom sexual reproduction

Diatoms rely on external environmental signals to initiate sexual reproduction, ensuring survival and genetic diversity.
These factors vary by species and habitat and include:

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
1. Cell size reduction

This is the primary factor that trigger the sexual reproduction in diatoms
Asexual reproduction (binary fission) gradually reduces cell size.
When cells reach a species-specific minimum size, they become sexually competent.
Example: Some Nitzschia species trigger sex at ~30% of original size.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
2. Light conditions

Photoperiod (day length): – many diatoms respond to seasonal light changes (e.g shorter days in autumn).
Example: marine centric diatoms like Thalassiosira reproduce sexually as daylight decreases.
Light quality – shifts in blue/green light spectra (e.g. underwater light gradients) can signal sexual reproduction.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
3. Nutrient availability

Silicon (Si) depletion: – diatoms require silica (Si) for frustule formation.
Low Si induces auxospore formation.
Example: Skeletonema marinoi initiates sexual reproduction during Si limitation.
Nitrogen (N) or Phosphorus (P) stress: – nutrient starvation accelerates size reduction, pushing cells toward sexual reproduction.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
4. Temperature shifts

Cooling or warming: – spring/autumn temperature changes synchronize sexual cycles.
Example: Pseudo-nitzschia blooms often transition to sexual reproduction after sudden cooling.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Ecological significance of diatom life cycle
text:
Ecological significance of diatom life cycle

Asexual phase: Enables rapid population growth during favorable conditions (blooms).
Sexual phase: Maintains genetic diversity and restores fitness.
Resting spores: Ensure survival through adverse periods and seed future blooms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Global importance

This life cycle strategy allows diatoms to dominate many aquatic ecosystems.
Diatoms contribute 20-50% of global ocean primary productivity.
Their unique reproductive strategy ensures both rapid growth and long-term survival.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Heteromorphy in diatoms

Heteromorphy refers to the phenomenon where a single diatom species produces morphologically different frustule forms at different stages of its life cycle.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Why does it happen?

Structural constraints:

Because the silica frustule is rigid, the cell cannot simply grow larger. Any change in volume or physiological state (like preparing for dormancy) requires the synthesis of a completely valve with a different architecture.

Functional adaptation:

Different forms serve different purposes. Vegetative cells are optimized for nutrient uptake and floating in the photic zone, while resting spores (a heteromorphic form) are optimized for sinking and protection against grazers or decay.

Genetic reset:

The initial cell formed after sexual reproduction often lacks the complex features (like specific spines or colony-linking structures) seen in mature vegetative cells, representing a morphological reset. This occurs primarily due to the size reduction-cycle and can involve changes in shape, ornamentation, or symmetry.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Heteromorphy
1. Life cycle-driven heteromorphy

Initial cells vs. vegetative cells

Initial cells (post-auxospore) have the largest and most symmetric frustules.
Vegetative cells (after repeated divisions) become smaller and may develop different shapes (e.g. more elongated or tapered).

Sexual vs. asexual phases

Some centric diatoms (e.g., Chaetoceros) produce resting spores with thick, heavily silicified walls, differing from their vegetative forms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes of Heteromorphy…
Environmental stress responses

Resting spores

Form under nutrient limitation, temperature shifts, or salinity changes.
Example: Thalassiosira produces spiny resting spores in winter.

Morphological plasticity: – Some diatom species alter valve structure in response to:

Low silica → Thinner, more porous frustules.
High predation pressure → Heavily armored forms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of heteromorphy
1. Size-dependent heteromorphy

Centric diatoms:

Early divisions produce radially symmetric cells.
Later divisions may shift toward oval or bipolar forms (e.g. Aulacoseira).

Pennate diatoms:

Example: In Nitzschia, smaller cells may develop more pronounced keels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of heteromorphy…
2. Sexual phase heteromorphy

Oogamous centrics:

Male (sperm-producing) and female (egg-producing) gametangia may have different frustule shapes.

Auxospore expansion:

Post-sexual initial cells often have unusual shapes before reverting to the species’s typical form.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton
Hormogonia – Motile trichomes of cells (used for dispersal) that are often gas-vacuolated and devoid of heterocysts, with a distinct cell shape and size compared to vegetative cells.





Oscillatoria species






Hormogonia of oscillatoria formed during vegetative reproduction

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Trichome is a chain of connected cells in filamentous cyanobacteria, functioning as a single physiological unit.

Trichomes are simply linear or coiled arrangements of cells within a shared sheath.
It includes the cell wall but not the sheath.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Features of Trichomes



Composed of a cell.
This can be uniform (e.g., Oscillatoria) or differentiated into specialized cells:

Vegetative cells: Perform photosynthesis.
Heterocysts: Fix nitrogen (thick-walled, pale).
Akinetes: Dormant cell (spores) (survival under stress).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Filament refers to a multicellular, thread-like structure composed of a linear chain of cells (trichome) enclosed within a protective mucilaginous sheath.

Filamentous cyanobacteria are among the most ecologically significant, as they often dominate blooms and contribute to nitrogen fixation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Heterocyst is a specialized, nitrogen-fixing cell found in filamentous cyanobacteria (e.g., Anabaena, Nostoc).

These cells are critical for converting atmospheric nitrogen (\(N_2\)) into ammonia (\(NH_3\)), a process called nitrogen fixation, which is essential for nutrient cycling in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Structural and Functional Features of Heterocysts
text:
Structural and Functional Features of Heterocysts

Thickened cell wall: Contains glycolipids to block oxygen (\(O_2\)) which inhibits nitrogenase, the enzyme for \(N_2\) fixation).
Pale appearance: Lacks phycobiliproteins (no photosynthesis) but retains chlorophyll-a.
Triggered by nitrogen limitation (e.g., low \(NO_3^-\) or \(NH_4^+\)).
No Photosystem II (PS-II): Prevents \(O_2\) production.
Uses Photosystem I (PS-I): Generates ATP for nitrogenase.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological importance of Heterocysts
Nitrogen Supply:

It provides fixed nitrogen (\(NH_3\)) to cyanobacteria and surrounding organisms.

Bloom formation:

It enables cyanobacteria (e.g., Anabaena) to dominate in nitrogen-poor waters.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Why are heterocysts oxygen-sensitive?

Nitrogenase, the enzyme that catalyzes nitrogen fixation (\(N_2 \rightarrow NH_3\)), is irreversibly inactivated/damaged by \(O_2\).
Therefore, heterocysts maintain an anaerobic environment by:

Blocking \(O_2\) diffusion (thickened wall).
Respiration: Uses \(O_2\) for ATP synthesis.
No PS-II: Prevents \(O_2\) generation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton

Akinete: Is a thick-walled, dormant spore formed by some filamentous cyanobacteria under unfavorable conditions (e.g., nutrient limitation, cold, or desiccation).
Akinetes allow survival in harsh environments and regenerate when conditions improve.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Structure of Akinetes

Enlarged cells: Larger than vegetative cells, with thickened cell walls.
Storage reserves: Accumulate glycogen, cyanophycin (nitrogen reserve), and lipids.
Pigments: Retain chlorophyll-a but reduce photosynthetic activity.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Formation of akinetes

Akinetes are formed if the conditions are unfavorable when there is:


Nutrient depletion (especially phosphorus or nitrogen).
Temperature shifts (cold or freezing).
Desiccation (prolonged dryness).
Light limitation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Germination of akinetes

When favorable conditions return:


Wall breaks down.
Cell divides to form a new filament.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vegetative cells

Vegetative cells are the primary photosynthetic cells in cyanobacteria, responsible for growth and energy production.
They contain chlorophyll-a and phycobiliproteins for photosynthesis and are typically thin-walled and actively dividing under favorable conditions.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Unicellular – Individual cells exist independently, often spherical or rod-shaped. Example: Chroococcus.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Colonial – Groups of cells are held together by a mucilaginous sheath but remain loosely associated. Example: Microcystis.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Pseudofilamentous – Cells form a chain-like arrangement but lack true cellular connections, appearing filamentous. Example: Merismopedia.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Filamentous – Cells form long, connected chains called trichomes, sharing a common sheath. Example: Oscillatoria.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



False Branching – Filaments appear branched but lack continuous cytoplasmic connections. Example: Scytonema.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



True Branching – Filaments divide in different directions with continuous cytoplasmic connections. Example: Stigonema.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of phytoplankton

Phytoplankton can be classified into three major groups which include the Cyanobacteria (Cyanophyta), Diatoms (Bacillariophyta), and Dinoflagellates (Dinophyta).
Some other groups includes Coccolithophores (Haptophyta), Green Algae (Chlorophyta), Cryptophytes (Cryptophyta).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria (Blue-green Algae)

Cyanobacteria are group of autotrophic, Gram-negative bacteria that obtain biological energy via oxygenic photosynthesis.
The name cyanobacteria originates from the Greek word kyanós, referring to their characteristic bluish-green (cyan) color.
This color forms the basis of their informal name, “blue-green algae,” although as prokaryotes, they are not scientifically classified as true algae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria (Blue-green algae)

They utilize photosynthetic pigments—including chlorophyll-a, carotenoids, and phycobilins (phycocyanin and phycoerythrin)—to harvest light energy.
Unlike most other bacteria, they possess an extensive system of internal membranes called thylakoids – flattened sacs, from which photosynthesis is performed.
They are the only prokaryotes capable of oxygenic photosynthesis, a process that significantly contributed to the oxygenation of Earth’s early atmosphere.
Many species produce potent cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystins, anatoxins) that pose significant risks to water quality, livestock, and human health.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria Orders
The Phylum Cyanobacteria is traditionally divided into several orders based on their morphological complexity:

Chroococcales: Unicellular or colonial forms; no filaments.
Oscillatoriales: Simple filamentous forms; no specialized cells (heterocysts/akinetes).
Nostocales: Filamentous forms with specialized cells (heterocysts and akinetes); false branching.
Stigonematales: Most complex; true branching and multiseriate filaments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria Orders…






Evolutionary Trend


The classification reflects an evolutionary progression from simple single cells to complex, multicellular-like structures with functional division of labor.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Chroococcales

The Chroococcales is an order of cyanobacteria known for including significant bloom-forming species like Microcystis aeruginosa.
They are primarily characterized by unicellular or colonial (non-filamentous) growth forms.
As one of the most ancient lineages, they are globally distributed across freshwater, brackish, and marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales


Morphology

They are unicellular (e.g., Synechococcus) or colonial (e.g., Microcystis).
Cells are typically spherical, oval, or rod-shaped.
Colony formations include:

Irregular clusters (Microcystis).
Cubical packets (Merismopedia).
Embedded in a gelatinous matrix (Gloeocapsa).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Cell Structure

Prokaryotic organization: Lack a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles.
Gram-negative cell wall: Composed of a peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane.
Mucilaginous sheath: A protective polysaccharide layer that aids in buoyancy and protection against desiccation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Reproduction

Reproduction by binary fission (asexual reproduction).
Colony fragmentation: Large colonies break into smaller aggregates to establish new populations.
Lack specialized reproductive cells (no akinetes or heterocysts).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Pigments & photosynthesis

They utilize chlorophyll-a alongside accessory phycobiliproteins (phycocyanin and phycoerythrin) to capture a broad spectrum of light.
They perform oxygenic photosynthesis within thylakoid membranes.
Unlike filamentous orders, they lack heterocysts; therefore, they cannot fix atmospheric nitrogen and rely on dissolved inorganic nitrogen (e.g., \(NO_3^-\), \(NH_4^+\)).
Many species (e.g., Microcystis) possess gas vacuoles to regulate their position in the water column for optimal light harvesting.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification of Chroococcales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common Genera & Species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological & economic importance of Chroococcales
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs):

Microcystis and Chroococcus can form dense surface scums that deplete oxygen and block light.
Microcystis produces microcystins, potent hepatotoxins (liver toxins) that threaten drinking water and aquatic life.

Primary Production:

Genera like Synechococcus are major contributors to global carbon fixation, especially in oligotrophic (nutrient-poor) oceans.

Bioindicators:

High abundance of Microcystis is often used as a bioindicator of eutrophication (excessive nutrient enrichment).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Oscillatoriales


The Oscillatoriales is an order of filamentous cyanobacteria distinguished by their structural simplicity:

Unbranched trichomes: Simple, linear chains of cells.
No specialized cells: They lack heterocysts and akinetes, relying on dissolved nitrogen.
Reproduction: Primarily through hormogonia (short motile segments) or filament fragmentation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Oscillatoriales


Morphology & structure

Unbranched trichomes: Simple linear chains of cells.
No specialized cells: Lack heterocysts and akinetes.
Cell Shape: Discoid or cylindrical with distinct cross-walls.
Sheath: Usually thin or absent, allowing for movement.

Cellular Composition

Pigments: Chlorophyll-a, phycocyanin, and phycoerythrin.
Prokaryotic: No membrane-bound organelles.


Motility & reproduction

Gliding movement: Active movement via mucilage secretion and contractile fibrils.
Fragmentation: Trichomes break into smaller pieces (hormogonia) to grow.
Hormogonia: Specialized short, motile segments used for dispersal.

Ecological niche

Often found in eutrophic (nutrient-rich) waters.
Can form dense benthic mats or surface blooms.








Key identification


The absence of heterocysts and akinetes in a filamentous cyanobacterium is a primary diagnostic feature of the Order Oscillatoriales, distinguishing them from the more complex Nostocales.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common genera & species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological & Economic Importance
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs):

Lyngbya produces lyngbyatoxin, a potent skin irritant causing “swimmers’ itch.”
Oscillatoria can dominate eutrophic waters, leading to oxygen depletion and foul odors.

Biofilms & Mats:

Phormidium forms dense benthic mats in freshwater, stabilizing sediments but potentially harboring toxins.

Commercial Uses:

Arthrospira (Spirulina) is globally cultivated as a nutrient-rich “superfood” due to its high protein and vitamin content.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Nostocales

The Nostocales are a morphologically diverse order of filamentous cyanobacteria characterized by the presence of specialized nitrogen-fixing cells (heterocysts) and often dormant spores (akinetes).
They play crucial ecological roles in nitrogen cycling and form symbiotic relationships with plants and fungi.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Morphology

Consists of a filamentous structure composed of trichomes (cell chains) enclosed in a mucilaginous sheath.
They have a specialized cells composed of:

Heterocysts: Thick-walled, pale cells for nitrogen fixation.
Akinetes: Enlarged, spore-like cells for survival under harsh environment.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Common growth forms include:

Linear filaments (Anabaena): Simple, straight chains of cells that often dominate open-water phytoplankton communities.
Coiled or Globose filaments (Nostoc): Filaments that twist or aggregate into gelatinous colonies, providing protection against grazing.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Cell Structure

Prokaryotic organization: Lack a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles.
Photosynthetic pigments:

Chlorophyll-a: Primary pigment for photosynthesis.
Phycobiliproteins: Accessory pigments (phycocyanin, allophycocyanin) for light harvesting.
Carotenoids: Provide photoprotection and light absorption.

Cell Wall: Gram-negative structure featuring a distinct peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales


Nitrogen Fixation

Heterocysts provide the low-oxygen conditions required for the nitrogenase enzyme to function.
Heterocyst positioning: – where these cells are located along the filament (trichome) can vary by species and environmental conditions and used to identify different genera:

Terminal: Located at the ends of filaments (e.g., Cylindrospermum).
Intercalary: Located between vegetative cells (e.g., Anabaena).
Suppression: Formation is often inhibited in nitrogen-rich environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Reproduction & dispersal

Binary fission: Standard cell division for filament elongation.
Fragmentation: Mechanical breaking of filaments into smaller colonies.
Hormogonia: Specialized, short, motile segments that detach for dispersal.
Akinetes: Thick-walled resting spores that ensure survival during environmental stress.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification of Nostocales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common genera & species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Stigonematales

The Stigonematales represent the most morphologically complex cyanobacteria, characterized by true branching filaments and multiseriate trichomes (filaments with cells arranged in multiple rows).
This order contains species with the highest degree of cellular differentiation among cyanobacteria.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales


Morphological complexity

True branching: Both T-type (transverse cell division) and Y-type (longitudinal division) branching
Multiseriate trichomes: Filaments composed of multiple cell rows (unlike other cyanobacteria)
Thick, laminated sheaths: Often pigmented (yellow, brown, or red)
Cell differentiation composed of:

Heterocysts (nitrogen-fixing cells)
Akinetes (resting spores)
Vegetative cells of varying shapes/sizes

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales
Cellular structure

Prokaryotic organization but with complex thylakoid arrangements
Pigments composed of:

Chlorophyll-a
Phycobiliproteins (phycocyanin dominant)
Unique carotenoids (e.g., nostoxanthin)

Cell walls: Thickened with additional structural layers

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales
Growth forms & habitat

Mat-forming species
Symbiotic species (with bryophytes)
Commonly found in moist soils, rocks, and thermal springs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Reproduction in Stigonematales

Hormogonia: Short, motile segments of the trichome that detach for dispersal and colonization.
Akinetes: Thick-walled resting spores that allow survival during periods of desiccation or nutrient stress.
Fragmentation: Mechanical breaking of the complex branched filaments into smaller viable units.
Binary Fission: Occurs in multiple planes, leading to the characteristic true branching and multiseriate (multiple rows) structure.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division

Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division



The ocean environment is divided based on:

Depth (vertical zones)
Distance from shore (horizontal zones)

These divisions help us understand the distribution of plankton and marine organisms
Each zone has unique characteristics affecting life forms





Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical Zones: Pelagic Division

Pelagic zone: The entire water column (open water)
Divided into distinct depth zones based on light penetration and pressure

Major Pelagic Zones:

Epipelagic (Euphotic) – 0-200 m
Mesopelagic (Twilight) – 200-1000 m
Bathypelagic (Midnight) – 1000-6000 m
Hadopelagic (Abyssal) – >6000 m

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Epipelagic Zone (Sunlight Zone)

Depth: 0-200 meters
Characteristics:

Receives sunlight
Warmest zone
Supports photosynthesis
Highest biodiversity
Dissolved oxygen abundant

Plankton: Rich in phytoplankton and zooplankton
Importance: Primary production occurs here

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Mesopelagic Zone (Twilight Zone)

Depth: 200-1000 meters
Characteristics:

Dim light (partial darkness)
Twilight conditions (not enough for photosynthesis)
Rapid temperature decrease
Lower photosynthesis

Plankton: Specialized zooplankton adapted to low light
Organisms: Bioluminescent species common

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Bathypelagic Zone (Midnight Zone)

Depth: 1000-6000 meters
Characteristics:

Complete darkness
Cold temperatures (2-4°C)
High pressure
Low oxygen in some regions

Plankton: Sparse plankton communities
Energy source: Marine snow (falling organic matter)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Hadopelagic Zone (Abyssal Zone)

Depth: >6000 meters
Characteristics:

Extreme darkness
Crushing pressure
Near-freezing temperatures
Minimal life

Plankton: Extremely specialized and rare
Organisms: Found near hydrothermal vents

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal Zones: Neritic vs. Oceanic
Neritic Province:

Shallow water above the continental shelf
Extends 200+ km from shore
High nutrient availability
High productivity
Supports rich plankton communities

Oceanic Province:

Deep water beyond the continental shelf
Low nutrient availability (except upwelling zones)
Lower productivity in many regions
Unique specialized plankton

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Benthic Division

Benthic zone: The seafloor and organisms living on/in it
Divided by depth:

Supralittoral – Above high tide
Littoral – Intertidal zone
Sublittoral – Continental shelf (< 200 m)
Abyssal – Deep ocean floor

Meroplankton larvae settle in benthic zones

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Key Factors Affecting Ocean Zones



Factor
Impact




Light
Enables photosynthesis; affects animal behavior


Temperature
Controls metabolic rates and organism distribution


Pressure
Affects gas solubility and organism physiology


Dissolved Oxygen
Essential for respiration; varies by depth


Nutrients
Crucial for primary production; limits growth


Salinity
Affects osmotic balance in organisms

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton Distribution by Zone


Epipelagic:

Rich plankton communities
Maximum phytoplankton
Diverse zooplankton
Larval forms

Mesopelagic:

Deep-sea copepods
Larval fishes
Myctophids (lanternfish)


Bathypelagic:

Specialized zooplankton
Detritus feeders
Bioluminescent species

Hadopelagic:

Hadal amphipods
Sparse copepods
Specialized bacteria

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Ocean Divisions

Scientific Understanding:

Predicting organism distribution
Understanding adaptation strategies
Tracking nutrient cycling

Conservation:

Managing marine resources
Understanding climate impacts
Protecting biodiversity

Fisheries:

Locating commercial species
Sustainable harvest practices

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Summary

Ocean divisions provide a framework for understanding marine ecosystems
Vertical divisions (epipelagic, mesopelagic, bathypelagic, hadopelagic) depend on depth and light
Horizontal divisions (neritic, oceanic) depend on distance from shore
Each zone has unique physical conditions
Plankton distributions reflect these environmental gradients

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Behavioral classification of zooplankton
While zooplankton are often grouped by size or taxonomy, their behavioral strategies are critical for understanding their ecological roles, survival, and distribution in the water column.
Key behavioral categories include:

Vertical migrators (Diel and seasonal)
Locomotion styles (Passive drifters vs. active swimmers)
Feeding strategies (Filter feeders vs. predators)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Diel Vertical Migration (DVM)

Diel migrators move vertically in the water column on a 24-hour cycle.
Strategy: They descend to deeper, darker waters during the day to avoid visual predators (like fish) and ascend to the nutrient-rich surface at night to feed on phytoplankton.
This is often considered the largest animal migration on Earth in terms of biomass.
Examples: Copepods, krill, and some species of jellyfish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Diel Vertical Migration (DVM)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Seasonal Migration

Seasonal migrators move vertically based on seasonal environmental shifts rather than daily light cycles.
Drivers: Changes in water temperature, nutrient availability, or life-cycle requirements (e.g., diapause).
Example: Many polar and temperate copepods migrate to deep water (ontogenetic migration) to overwinter in a state of reduced metabolism when surface food is scarce.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Seasonal Migration

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Passive Drifters

Passive drifters: Organisms that lack the ability to swim against major horizontal currents and rely entirely on water movement for dispersal.
Ecological role: This strategy is common among meroplankton, allowing the offspring of benthic or nektonic adults to colonize new habitats.
Examples:

Larvae of many marine organisms (crabs, lobsters, fish).
Large gelatinous organisms like some jellyfish (though they can pulse, they cannot overcome strong currents).



Passive Drifters

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Active Swimmers

Active swimmers: Zooplankton that possess the musculature and specialized appendages to move independently of small-scale water turbulence.
Mechanisms:

Use of appendages (e.g., copepod swimming legs, krill pleopods).
Jet propulsion (e.g., pulsing of jellyfish medusae or salp contractions).
Undulatory movement (e.g., arrow worms).

Purpose: Allows for directed movement to find food patches, locate mates, and perform rapid escape responses from predators.
Examples: Chaetognaths (arrow worms), euphausiids (krill), and many jellyfish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Strategies


Filter Feeders

These are zooplankton that feed by filtering small particles (phytoplankton, bacteria, detritus) from the water column.
They use specialized structures such as cilia (rotifers), setae (copepods), or bristles to capture food particles.
This strategy is essential for transferring energy from primary producers to higher trophic levels.
Examples: Copepods, krill, salps, and rotifers.





Filter feeding mechanism

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Strategies


Predators

These are zooplankton that actively hunt and consume other planktonic organisms.
They have specialized structures such as hooks, spines, or tentacles to capture their prey.
They play a top-down role in regulating the abundance of other zooplankton and fish larvae.
Examples: Jellyfish, chaetognaths (arrow worms), and some predatory copepods.





Predation feeding

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Genetic Classification of Zooplankton

Genetic tools are essential for resolving evolutionary relationships and assessing biodiversity that morphological studies might miss.


Phylogenetic classification:

Based on genetic relatedness using molecular techniques like DNA sequencing.
Helps determine evolutionary lineages and has led to the revision of many taxonomic groups (e.g., identifying cryptic species in copepods).

Population genetics:

Studies genetic variation within and between populations.
Used to track the health of krill populations and how they adapt to changing ocean environments.

Functional genetics:

Focuses on the genetic basis of traits like swimming behavior, feeding efficiency, and predator avoidance.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Behavior
Zooplankton employ diverse strategies to acquire energy:



Filter feeding: The most common strategy. Uses cilia, setae, or bristles to sieve particles (algae, bacteria) from water.

Examples: Copepods, krill, salps.






Filter feeding

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Behavior…

Grazing: Scraping or grazing on surfaces (plants/rocks) to consume diatoms and larger phytoplankton.

Examples: Rotifers, some copepods.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Behavior…



Predation: Active hunting using hooks, spines, or tentacles.

Examples: Jellyfish, arrow worms.






Predation feeding

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Behavior…



Mixotrophy: Combining photosynthesis with heterotrophic feeding.

Examples: Some dinoflagellates and ciliates.






Mixotrophyic feeding

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Feeding Behavior…

Parasitism: Living off the fluids or tissues of a host.

Examples: Parasitic copepods.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Reproduction

Zooplankton have a variety of reproductive strategies, ranging from sexual reproduction to asexual reproduction.
Some species reproduce sexually, with males and females releasing their gametes into the water column.
Other species reproduce asexually, with individuals producing clones of themselves through budding or fragmentation.
In some cases, zooplankton can switch between sexual and asexual reproduction depending on environmental conditions i.e Daphnia, Jellyfish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Sexual reproduction

Many species of zooplankton reproduce sexually, which involves the fusion of gametes (sperm and egg cells).
In some species, males and females release their gametes into the water, where fertilization occurs externally.
In other species, males transfer their sperm to the female, who then fertilizes her eggs internally.
Examples of zooplankton with sexual reproduction are Copepods and Krill.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Asexual reproduction

Some species of zooplankton are capable of asexual reproduction, where offspring are produced without the involvement of gametes.
This can occur through various mechanisms such as budding, fragmentation, or parthenogenesis (where an unfertilized egg develops into an embryo).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Asexual reproduction…

Budding: A new individual grows as an outgrowth from the parent organism, which then detaches and becomes a separate individual.

Examples: Cnidaria and Scyphozoans.



Cnidaria and Scyphozoan

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Asexual reproduction…

Fragmentation: The parent organism breaks into several parts, each of which can grow into a new individual.
Parthenogenesis: A type of reproduction in which an unfertilized egg develops into a new individual without fertilization.

Example: Some species of rotifers reproduce through parthenogenesis and produce only females.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Mixis

Mixis is a specialized reproductive strategy where environmental cues (e.g., photoperiod, temperature, or population density) trigger a switch from asexual to sexual reproduction.
This process results in the production of resting eggs, which are thick-walled and highly resistant to environmental stress.
Ecological significance: These eggs can remain dormant in the sediment for years, acting as a seed bank to repopulate the environment when favorable conditions return.
Example: Rotifers.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Hermaphroditism

Hermaphroditism: Some species possess both male and female reproductive organs.

This allows for self-fertilization or mating with any other individual of the same species, maximizing reproductive opportunities in sparse populations.
It helps increase or maintain genetic diversity through cross-fertilization.
Examples: Arrow worms (Chaetognaths) and some species of Ctenophores.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Life cycle of zooplankton

The life cycle of zooplankton typically includes several stages, starting from the egg stage, followed by larval stages, and finally reaching adulthood.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Life Cycle: From Egg to Larva
text:
Life Cycle: From Egg to Larva

The egg stage: Females produce large numbers of small, transparent eggs. These are often buoyant and carried long distances by ocean currents.
Hatching: Once the eggs hatch, the larvae emerge.
Larval characteristics:

Often look completely different from adults (morphologically distinct).
May have different feeding habits and behaviors.
Some are active swimmers, while others are passive drifters.
This stage is critical for rapid growth and energy acquisition.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Life Cycle: Juvenile to Adult

Juvenile stage: As larvae develop, they transition into juveniles that begin to resemble the adult form.
During this stage, they undergo significant morphological changes, such as the development of appendages for swimming and feeding.

Development of specialized feeding structures.
Improved swimming efficiency.
Survival challenge: Must avoid intense predation while finding enough food to reach maturity.

Adult stage: The final stage where the organism is sexually mature.

Range from microscopic copepods to massive jellyfish.
Occupy specific ecological niches (filter feeders vs. predators).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental Timing of Reproduction

Reproduction is rarely random; it is often synchronized with environmental cues:

Temperature: Warmer waters often accelerate metabolic and reproductive rates.
Light: Photoperiod changes can trigger spawning or mixis.
Nutrient availability: Reproduction often peaks during phytoplankton blooms to ensure larvae have adequate food.
Example: Many species reproduce more intensely during summer when food supply and temperatures are optimal.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Affecting Zooplankton

Spatial and seasonal changes of zooplankton are a critical aspect of marine ecology.
Zooplankton are the primary consumers in the marine food web and play a crucial role in the transfer of energy from phytoplankton to higher trophic levels.
The spatial and temporal distribution of zooplankton is influenced by a variety of factors, including physical and chemical conditions, as well as biological interactions.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Physical and Chemical Factors
Environmental Drivers:

Temperature: Influences metabolic rates, growth, and thermal niche boundaries.
Light: Dictates DVM patterns and visibility to predators.
Salinity & pH: Affects osmotic balance and physiology (especially for calcifying groups like Foraminifera).
Ocean currents: Primary mechanism for the dispersal of passive drifters and meroplankton.
Nutrients: Indirectly control zooplankton via the abundance of their prey (phytoplankton/bacteria).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Physical and Chemical Factors…
Spatial Distribution:

Upwelling zones: High nutrient availability leads to phytoplankton blooms, supporting massive zooplankton biomass.
Estuaries: High productivity but challenging salinity gradients; favors specialized species.
Vertical stratification: Temperature and density layers (thermoclines) can act as barriers or aggregation points for different species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Physical and Chemical Factors…
Temporal (Seasonal) Changes:

Spring/Summer: Explosive growth triggered by warming waters and the spring phytoplankton bloom.
Autumn: Populations decline as temperatures cool, food becomes scarce, and predation pressure peaks.
Winter: Many species enter dormancy (diapause) or produce resting eggs to survive low-food conditions.
Chemical stressors: Sensitivity to heavy metals (e.g., copper, mercury) and acidification can limit survival in polluted or high-CO₂ environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Biological Factors
Biological interactions significantly shape zooplankton distribution:

Predation: Consumption by fish, whales, or larger invertebrates. Predators create landscapes of fear where zooplankton distribution is dictated by predator avoidance behaviors.
Grazing Pressure: Zooplankton abundance is often inversely related to phytoplankton density (top-down control).
Competition: Interspecific and intraspecific competition for limited resources like food or optimal habitat.
Reproductive Rates: Population spikes are tied to the growth rates of their prey (bottom-up control).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental Sensitivity

Zooplankton are also sensitive to their environment.
Bio-indicators: A change in zooplankton concentration or community structure can indicate subtle environmental shifts.
Responsiveness: They are highly responsive to nutrient levels, temperature changes, pollution, poor food quality, light levels, and predation pressure.
Ecosystem health: The diversity of species, biomass, and abundance of zooplankton communities are used as metrics to determine the overall health and productivity of an ecosystem.
Early warning: Because of their short life cycles, they respond rapidly to stressors, acting as an early warning system for environmental degradation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Applications in Scientific Research
Zooplankton serve as vital tools across multiple scientific disciplines:

Ecological studies: Used to assess ecosystem health, productivity, and energy transfer dynamics.
Climate research: Act as sentinels of climate change. Shifts in the timing of zooplankton blooms (phenology) indicate warming trends and impact the entire food web.
Aquaculture: Essential as live feed for larval fish and shellfish. Research focuses on sustainable mass-culturing techniques.
Biotechnology: Source of biologically active compounds for medicine (e.g., anti-inflammatory or antimicrobial agents).
Water quality monitoring: Serve as bio-indicators. Their sensitivity to pollutants and pH makes them ideal for tracking environmental degradation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Emerging Technologies in Zooplankton Research
Modern methods are revolutionizing how we study these drifters:

High-resolution imaging: Underwater cameras and ROVs allow for real-time observation of behavior in natural habitats.
Environmental DNA (eDNA) & Metabarcoding: Identifying species from water samples without needing to capture individual organisms.
Acoustic methods: Using echosounders to quantify biomass and track DVM across entire ocean basins.
Stable isotope analysis: Measuring Carbon and Nitrogen isotopes to map complex who-eats-whom relationships.
Machine learning (AI): Automated image recognition to classify thousands of plankton samples in seconds.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Importance Summary

Trophic transfer: The bridge between primary producers and megafauna.
Nutrient cycling: Rapid recycling of Nitrogen and Phosphorus in the photic zone.
Carbon sequestration: Driving the Biological Pump by transporting carbon to the deep sea via fecal pellets and sinking carcasses.
Oxygen balance: Contribution to global oxygen levels through mixotrophic and photosynthetic activity.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Conservation and Management
Protecting zooplankton is fundamental to ocean conservation:

Pollution reduction: Minimizing plastic and chemical runoff that impairs reproduction.
Fisheries management: Ensuring forage fish harvests don’t deplete the food source for higher trophic levels.
Habitat protection: Safeguarding nurseries like mangroves and seagrass beds where meroplankton thrive.
Global monitoring: Supporting long-term datasets to track the impacts of ocean acidification.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production

Primary production is the creation of new organic matter from inorganic substrates.
This organic matter serves as the base of the food web for most marine ecosystems.
Primary production generally refers to the process of photosynthesis, or the utilization of light energy to produce chemical energy.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

The photosynthesis process is undertaken by plants and algae according to the reaction below:

\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow[\text{Chlorophyll}]{\text{Light}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\]

However, primary production is also carried out by bacteria in the absence of light through chemosynthesis.
Instead of light providing the energy for the reaction, the energy comes from the oxidation of inorganic materials, such as hydrogen sulfide on hydrothermal vents

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

Primary production in the ocean is carried out by marine plants i.e mangroves, seagrass, seaweed, and phytoplankton, with phytoplankton being the most important contributors to global primary production.
The greatest amount of primary production in the ocean comes from the phytoplankton
The total amount of organic material created by the producers is called the gross primary productivity, or total production.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

However, the primary producers consume a portion of this organic matter themselves through respiration in order to sustain their life.
So, the total amount that is left to support the consumers in the ocean is called net production

\[Net~production=Gross~production – Respiration\]

The net production is the amount of organic matter that is available to support the consumers in the ocean.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

The gross production is the total amount of organic matter created by the producers, while respiration is the amount of organic matter consumed by the producers themselves.
Gross production can be divided into two components; new production and regenerated production.
New production is supported by nutrients brought in from outside of the local ecosystem through processes such as upwelling or ocean currents.
Regenerated production results from the recycling of nutrients within an ecosystem.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

Most primary producers require nitrogen and phosphorus, which are available in the ocean as nitrate, nitrite, ammonia, and phosphate.
The abundances of these molecules and the intensity and quality of light exert a major influence on rates of production.
The two principal categories of producers (autotrophs) in the sea are phytoplankton and benthic microalgae and macroalgae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Production…

Benthic plants grow only on the fringe of the world’s oceans and are estimated to produce only 5 to 10 percent of the total marine plant material in a year
Chemoautotrophs are the producers of the deep-sea vents.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Measurements of primary production

Primary productivity is usually determined by measuring the uptake of carbon dioxide or the output of oxygen.
Production rates are usually expressed as grams of organic carbon per unit area per unit time (\(gC/m^2/yr\))

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Oxygen emission method

The oxygen emission method is one of the commonly used procedures for measuring primary production in the ocean.
The method involves measuring the rate of oxygen production by phytoplankton as a result of photosynthesis.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
Sampling:

A water sample is collected from the ocean using two sampling bottle or Niskin bottle; a transparent bottle and a dark bottle.
The sample should be collected at a certain depth where photosynthesis is occurring and the water must not be too turbid.
The dark bottled sample will be used as a control in the experiment

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
Incubation:

The two water samples are placed in a dark, temperature-controlled incubator and exposed to light of a known intensity for a specific period of time (usually 1-24 hours).
During this time, the phytoplankton in the transparent bottle will undergo photosynthesis and produce oxygen.
The phytoplankton in the dark bottle will not undergo photosynthesis, rather respiration due to insufficient light. So, they will consume the available oxygen in the sample

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
Oxygen measurement:

After the incubation period, the amount of dissolved oxygen in both the transparent bottle (O2) and dark bottle (O3) sample will be measured using a dissolved oxygen probe or titration method.

Oxygen correction:

The amount of oxygen produced by photosynthesis is calculated by subtracting the amount of oxygen consumed by respiration during the incubation period.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
The components of primary production are calculated as follows:
Respiration (R) \(= O_1 - O_3\)
Net Primary Production (NPP) \(= O_2 - O_1\)
Gross Primary Production (GPP) \(= NPP + R\) (or \(O_2 - O_3\))

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
Where;
\(O_1\) =Initial oxygen concentration
\(O_2\) = Amount of dissolved oxygen in transparent bottle after incubation
\(O_3\) = Amount of dissolved oxygen in dark bottle after incubation

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Steps in oxygen method…
Calculation:

The rate of primary production is calculated as the amount of oxygen produced per unit of time (usually milligrams of oxygen per cubic meter of water per hour).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations on primary production
Qn1.
In a light-dark bottle experiment, the initial DO was 8 mg/L. After 24 hours, the light bottle had 10 mg/L DO, and the dark bottle had 6 mg/L DO. Calculate GPP, NPP, and R.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations…
Answer:
Respiration (R) = Initial DO − Dark bottle DO = 8 − 6 = 2 mg/L/day
Net Primary Production (NPP) = Light bottle DO − Initial DO = 10 − 8 = 2 mg/L/day
Gross Primary Production (GPP) = NPP + R = 2 + 2 = 4 mg/L/day

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations…
Qn2
If the oxygen evolution rate (Gross Production) in a phytoplankton sample is 0.5 mg O₂/L/h in light conditions and the respiration rate is 0.2 mg O₂/L/h, what is the net primary production rate?

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations…
Answer:
NPP = GPP − Respiration

\(0.5\text{ mg O}_2/\text{L/h} - 0.2\text{ mg O}_2/\text{L/h} = 0.3\text{ mg O}_2/\text{L/h}\)

The net primary production rate is 0.3 mg O₂/L/h. This represents the actual biomass increase available to the next trophic level after the phytoplankton have met their own metabolic energy demands.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations…
Qn3
A lake water sample showed an initial DO of 7.2 mg/L. After 6 hours in the light, DO increased to 8.4 mg/L, while in the dark, it decreased to 6.5 mg/L. Compute GPP, NPP, and R per day (assuming constant rates).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Calculations…
Answer:
Respiration (R) = (Initial − Dark) × (24/6) = (7.2 − 6.5) × 4 = 2.8 mg/L/day
NPP = (Light − Initial) × (24/6) = (8.4 − 7.2) × 4 = 4.8 mg/L/day
GPP = NPP + R = 4.8 + 2.8 = 7.6 mg/L/day

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Objectives
To introduce students to the systematics of both phytoplankton and zooplankton; plankton sampling methods and determination of productivity; plankton ecology and productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Synopsis/Modules

Module 1: Course Introduction

Introduction to plankton and their importance in aquatic ecosystems.
Classification of Plankton
Division of the aquatic environment



Module 2: Plankton Ecology

Systematic and biology of phytoplankton and Zooplankton (holoplankton and meroplankton).
Adaptation to the environment:

Structural adaptations to floatation, aids of floatation
Cyclomorphosis, colour and transparency, bioluminescence




Module 3: Plankton Productivity

Primary production in the aquatic environment.
Secondary production and the food webs.
Bacterial production and the concept of the microbial loop.
Factors controlling the distribution and productivity.
Nutrient regeneration and the role of zooplankton in nutrient cycling.
Trophodynamic relationships.
Feeding processes: rate of filtering, prey selection.
Zooplankton variations and the concept of indicator species.
Zoogeography of the holoplankton.
Vertical migration: seasonal, ontogenic and diel-vertical migration.
Spatial and seasonal changes of phytoplankton and zooplankton.



Module 4: Harmful Microalgae

Harmful algal blooms (HABs): causes, effects, and management strategies.
Types of HABs



Module 5: Sampling Methods and Laboratory Analysis

Methods of measurement of biomass and primary production, bacterial production, and nitrogen fixation in the water column.
Quantitative phytoplankton and zooplankton sampling; methods of collecting, fixing and preserving.
Laboratory analyses of planktons.
