title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Brown tide
Causative organisms:

Primarily caused by the rapid growth of specific species belonging to the class Pelagophyceae:

Aureococcus anophagefferens: Known for maintaining high growth efficiency even under nutrient limitation.
Aureomonas lagunensis: Tolerates wide temperature and salinity ranges, with accelerated growth at higher temperatures.


Characteristics:

Brown tide algae do not produce toxins that directly harm humans or animals.
The name brown tide refers to the characteristic brownish coloration of the water caused by the abundance of algae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Brown tide…

Brown tide

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological effects of Brown tide
Reduced light penetration:

High cell densities (up to \(10^9\) cells/L) significantly increase turbidity.
Shading inhibits the growth of submerged aquatic vegetation (SAV), particularly seagrasses like Zostera marina.
Loss of seagrass leads to habitat degradation for juvenile fish and invertebrates.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological effects of Brown tide…
Impact on shellfish (indirect toxicity):

While non-toxic to humans, these algae interfere with the feeding mechanisms of filter-feeders.
Mechanical interference: High concentrations clog the cilia of shellfish (e.g., scallops and mussels).
Nutritional deficiency: Poor food quality leads to starvation, reduced growth, and mass recruitment failure in bivalve populations.
Trophic inhibition: Reduced light penetration can inhibit the growth of other phytoplankton that shellfish depend on for food.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological effects of Brown tide…
Altered food web dynamics:

Brown tide blooms alter the composition of the plankton community, causing cascading effects.
Zooplankton inhibition: The small size and poor nutritional quality of Aureococcus can inhibit grazing by copepods.
Changes in plankton populations affect the abundance and distribution of higher trophic levels (fish and marine mammals) that rely on them for food.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Economic effects of Brown tide

Fisheries and aquaculture: Significant financial losses for fishermen and shellfish farmers due to mass recruitment failure and reduced growth in bivalve populations (e.g., scallops and mussels).
Industry closures: Mandatory closures of shellfish harvesting areas due to contamination concerns and population declines.
Tourism and recreation: The characteristic brownish discoloration and loss of seagrass habitats can negatively impact coastal tourism and recreational activities.
Cascading costs: Economic impacts extend to businesses reliant on healthy marine ecosystems, such as local seafood markets and tourism-dependent services.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Red tide
Causative organisms:

Red tides are phenomena primarily caused by dinoflagellates.
Common species include Karenia brevis (prevalent in the Gulf of Mexico) and Alexandrium spp.
These organisms produce potent toxins such as brevetoxins and saxitoxins.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of red tide

Despite the name, water discoloration can range from reddish-brown to yellow or green, depending on the pigments of the dominant species.
The water often appears murky or “rusty” due to extremely high cell concentrations.
Toxins can bioaccumulate through the food web, eventually reaching humans via contaminated seafood.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Red tide…

Red tide

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Effects of red tide
Toxic effects:

The toxins produced by red tide algae can have harmful effects on marine life.

Direct mortality: Toxins can kill fish, shellfish, and other marine organisms through acute poisoning.
Indirect mortality: Massive blooms can cause oxygen depletion (hypoxia) in the water column during decomposition, leading to suffocation of marine life.
Bioaccumulation: Marine mammals, seabirds, and turtles can be affected or killed after consuming contaminated prey, moving toxins up the food chain.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Effects of red tide…
Human health risks:

Exposure routes: Toxins pose risks through the consumption of contaminated seafood or the inhalation of aerosolized toxins (common with Karenia brevis).
Symptom range: Ingestion can cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and severe neurological effects.
Specific syndromes: Red tides are frequently associated with Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) and Neurotoxic Shellfish Poisoning (NSP).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP)

Causative organisms: Produced by dinoflagellate species including Alexandrium spp. (e.g., A. tamarense, A. catenella), Gymnodinium catenatum, and Pyrodinium bahamense.
Transmission: Induced via consuming contaminated bivalve molluscs (mussels, clams), crustacea, or gastropods that have filtered or fed on toxic cells.
Severity: A serious, potentially fatal illness characterized by rapid onset of neurological symptoms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
PSP…
PSP toxin(s)

The primary biotoxin responsible is SAXITOXIN (STX).
It is considered one of the most potent natural neurotoxins known.
Mechanism: It binds to voltage-gated sodium channels in nerve cells, blocking nerve impulse transmission and leading to muscle paralysis.
Common derivatives include neosaxitoxin (neoSTX), gonyautoxins (GTX), and decarbamoylsaxitoxin (dcSTX), which vary in toxicity levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
PSP Symptoms

Early signs: Tingling or numbness in the face, lips, tongue, and extremities.
Progression: Followed by headache, dizziness, nausea, and loss of muscle coordination (ataxia).
Severe cases: Progressive muscular paralysis and respiratory failure.
Fatality: Death can occur within 2 to 12 hours after consumption due to respiratory arrest.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
PSP Symptoms…

Recovery: If the dose is low and medical support (like mechanical ventilation) is provided, symptoms typically diminish within 9 hours.
Prognosis: Patients who survive the first 24 hours have a high probability of a full and rapid recovery, as the toxin is eventually excreted.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP)

Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP) is a type of foodborne illness caused by consuming shellfish contaminated with lipophilic toxins produced by harmful algal species, particularly dinoflagellates.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP)
Causative organisms & toxins:

Main toxin producers include:

Dinophysis spp. (e.g., D. acuminata, D. fortii)
Prorocentrum spp. (e.g., P. lima)

Toxins involved are:

Okadaic Acid (OA): The most common DSP toxin.
Dinophysistoxins (DTXs).
Pectenotoxins (PTXs): Often co-occur, though less diarrheagenic.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
DSP…
Mechanism of action:

These toxins are lipophilic and inhibit protein phosphatases, leading to hyperphosphorylation of proteins in intestinal cells, resulting in intestinal fluid secretion and severe diarrhea.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Symptoms of DSP
Clinical presentation:

Onset: Symptoms typically begin between 30 minutes to 12 hours after ingestion.
Primary symptoms:

Severe diarrhea (most characteristic sign).
Nausea and vomiting.
Abdominal cramps.
Mild fever (rare).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Symptoms of DSP…

Duration: Symptoms usually resolve within 3 days without long-term sequelae.

Sources of contamination:

Primarily through the consumption of affected filter-feeding shellfish such as mussels, clams, oysters, and scallops that accumulate toxins in their digestive glands.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Economic and Environmental Impacts
Economic impacts:

Fisheries & Aquaculture: Mass fish kills and mandatory shellfish closures lead to direct financial losses for fishermen and farmers.
Tourism: Beach closures and the presence of dead marine life deter visitors, impacting hotels, restaurants, and local businesses.
Public Health Costs: Increased medical expenses and lost workdays due to respiratory issues or seafood poisoning.
Management Costs: Expenses related to water quality monitoring, toxin testing, and carcass cleanup.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental Impacts
}

Red tide blooms can disrupt marine ecosystems by causing mass mortalities of fish, shellfish, and other marine organisms.
They can also harm coral reefs and seagrass beds by reducing light penetration and depleting oxygen levels in the water.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Monitoring and Management of red tide
text:
Monitoring and Management of red tide

Monitoring programs are essential for tracking the development and movement of red tide blooms and assessing their potential impacts on human health and the environment.
Management strategies may include the closure of shellfish harvesting areas, public health advisories, and research into methods for mitigating or preventing red tide blooms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Green tide

A green tide refers to a massive proliferation of green phytoplankton in marine or freshwater ecosystems, leading to visible green discoloration of the water.
These events are often caused by nutrient enrichment (eutrophication), particularly from excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff, sewage, or industrial discharges.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Green tide…

It leads to overgrowth of cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) particularly Microcystis, Spirogyra.
Green tide blooms can give the water a greenish colour, hence the name.
The algae may form dense mats or carpets on the water surface or accumulate along shorelines, especially in areas with low water circulation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Green tide…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Causes & effects

Eutrophication (excessive nutrients like nitrogen & phosphorus) particularly from agricultural runoff, sewage discharges, and urban stormwater runoff.
Warm temperatures and calm water conditions.
Some species may produce toxins harmful to humans and animals.
it may also cause skin irritation, respiratory issues, and poisoning if ingested.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Impacts

Not all green algae are toxic, excessive growth of green tide can have negative ecological impacts.
Dense mats of algae can block sunlight from reaching submerged aquatic vegetation, such as seagrasses, which are important habitats for many marine species.
This reduction in light penetration can disrupt the balance of the ecosystem and lead to declines in biodiversity.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Zooplankton?

The term zooplankton originates from the Greek words zoon (animal) and planktos (wanderer or drifter).
They are heterotrophic aquatic organisms, ranging from microscopic single-celled protozoans to large multicellular animals like jellyfish.
Unlike nekton (active swimmers like fish), zooplankton are primarily drifters that move with water currents, though many exhibit significant vertical swimming capabilities.
They occupy a central position in aquatic ecosystems, acting as the primary link between microscopic primary producers (phytoplankton) and higher trophic levels.







Note


Zooplankton are not a single taxonomic group but an ecological category defined by their lifestyle.
They include members from multiple phyla across the animal kingdom, as well as some protists.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Zooplankton…

The zooplankton community is composed of both primary consumers, which feed on phytoplankton, and secondary consumers, which feed on other zooplankton.
This diverse group includes Protozoa, Metazoa, crustaceans, rotifers, open-water insect larvae, and aquatic mites.
They serve as a vital link in the food web, transferring energy from primary producers to higher trophic levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Zooplankton…

While most are microscopic, some (like the jellyfish) can reach several meters in length.
They include single-celled organisms like zooflagellates, foraminiferans, and radiolarians, and multicellular animals such as pelagic cnidarians, ctenophores, molluscs, arthropods, and tunicates.
Despite their name, many can swim (e.g., vertical migration), but they cannot swim against major horizontal currents.







Note


Zooplankton span over 12 orders of magnitude in body size, from tiny protists to giant jellyfish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Zooplankton…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton
Zooplankton are fundamental to aquatic ecosystems, serving as the “engine” of energy transfer and nutrient cycling. Their importance is categorized into four key roles:
1. Foundation of the food web

Trophic link: They serve as the primary intermediate species, transferring energy from microscopic phytoplankton to larger invertebrate predators and fish.
Primary consumers: By grazing on phytoplankton, they convert solar energy stored in plant biomass into animal tissue accessible to higher trophic levels.
Energy conduit: Without this link, the energy produced by phytoplankton would be largely unavailable to the vast majority of marine and freshwater megafauna.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton

In turn, zooplankton become food for a vast array of larger organisms, including;

Small fish (like sardines and herring)
Larval stages of many fish and invertebrates
Shellfish (like krill, which are a type of zooplankton themselves, but also feed on smaller zooplankton)
Some seabirds
Even giant filter-feeders like baleen whales (blue whales, humpback whales) and whale sharks.

Without zooplankton, many of these larger animals would not have a sufficient food source, leading to a collapse of the entire ecosystem.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton…
2. Nutrient cycling

Zooplankton play a vital role in recycling nutrients.
As they consume phytoplankton and organic matter, they excrete waste products like ammonia and phosphate directly into the photic zone.
These waste products are essential limiting nutrients that phytoplankton immediately reuse for growth, fueling further primary production.
When predators consume zooplankton, they acquire nutrients originally synthesized by phytoplankton, moving them higher up the food web.
This process is a key component of biogeochemical cycles, ensuring that essential elements are not lost but continuously recycled within the aquatic environment.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton…
3. Biological carbon pump

Zooplankton contribute to the biological carbon pump.
They consume large amounts of carbon-rich phytoplankton and other organic matter, which they then excrete as fecal pellets.
These dense pellets sink rapidly, bypassing the surface recycling processes.
When zooplankton die or perform diel vertical migration, they transport carbon from the surface to the deep ocean.
This helps to sequester carbon in the deep ocean, playing a role in regulating the Earth’s climate.
This process effectively “pumps” atmospheric CO₂ into long-term storage in deep-sea sediments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton…
4.Indicators of ecosystem health

Bio-indicators: Because zooplankton are sensitive to changes in water quality, temperature, salinity, and pollution, their community structure serves as a “canary in the coal mine” for aquatic health.
Environmental monitoring: Scientists track shifts in abundance and diversity to detect the early impacts of climate change, eutrophication, and chemical stressors.
Disturbance detection: Environmental disturbances are often first visible through changes in species composition, body size distribution, and the timing of seasonal blooms.
Research applications: They are used in ecological studies, water quality monitoring, and even biotechnology for drug development from biologically active compounds.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Zooplankton…

Common Zooplankton Species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Taxonomy and Systematics

Taxonomy is the science of naming, defining, and classifying groups of biological organisms on the basis of shared characteristics.
Systematics is a broader field that studies the diversification of living forms and their evolutionary relationships (phylogeny).
Zooplankton are defined by their ecological role (heterotrophic, drifting animals) rather than a shared evolutionary lineage.
This means zooplankton are incredibly diverse and polyphyletic, originating from many different phyla across the animal kingdom.







Tip


Because they are grouped by how they live rather than who they are related to, zooplankton taxonomy covers everything from single-celled protists to complex vertebrates.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Taxonomy and Systematics…

Zooplankton can be classified by either their size and/or by their developmental stage (life cycle).
Size Classification:

Picoplankton (< 2 \(\mu\)m): Mostly bacteria and very small protists.
Nanoplankton (2–20 \(\mu\)m): Small flagellates.
Microplankton (20–200 \(\mu\)m): Ciliates, rotifers, and nauplii.
Mesoplankton (0.2–20 mm): Copepods, cladocerans, and small larvae.
Macroplankton (2–20 cm): Krill, small jellyfish, and arrow worms.
Megaplankton (> 20 cm): Large jellyfish and colonial salps.

There are two categories used to classify zooplankton by their stage of development: meroplankton and holoplankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Taxonomy and Systematics…

Zooplankton are also classified by their developmental strategy:


Meroplankton (Temporary Plankton):

Organisms that spend only part of their life cycle (usually the larval stage) as plankton.
As they mature, they settle to the bottom (benthic) or become active swimmers (nektonic).
Examples: Larvae of crabs, lobsters, fish, corals, and sea urchins.

Holoplankton (Permanent Plankton):

Organisms that spend their entire life cycle drifting in the water column.
Examples: Copepods, krill, chaetognaths, and salps.








Important


Meroplankton and holoplankton are found in almost every major taxonomic group, representing a diverse array of evolutionary adaptations to a drifting lifestyle.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Taxonomy and Systematics…

Some amoebas such as those classified as Foraminifera and Actinopoda have hard skeletons, usually larger than 2 millimeters in diameter, that help form deep-sea sediment.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Major taxonomic groups in zooplankton

Protozoan Zooplankton (Single-celled Eukaryotes)


These are often referred to as microzooplankton due to their small size.
They include four major groups;


Foraminifera


Radiolaria


Ciliates


Heterotrophic dinoflagellates

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
i. Foraminifera and Radiolaria
Phylum Retaria

Majority are marine, inhabiting all oceans from surface waters to the deep sea. Some foraminiferans occur in brackish or freshwater.
They possess specialized pseudopods and often intricate spines that aid in buoyancy and prey capture.
While many are planktonic, numerous species are benthic, living on or within seafloor sediments.
Their discarded shells (tests) accumulate over millions of years, forming significant portions of deep-sea sediment and providing a vital fossil record for paleoclimatology.







Note


Retaria includes the Foraminifera and Radiolaria, which are among the most important groups for studying Earth’s historical climate.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Retaria

Amoeboid protists with specialized pseudopods:


These organisms are fundamentally amoeboid, meaning they can change their shape and move using cytoplasmic extensions called pseudopods.
A distinctive feature of Phylum Retaria is the presence of two specialized types of pseudopods: reticulopodia (found in Foraminifera) and axopodia (found in Radiolaria).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Retaria…

Reticulopodia (Foraminifera): These are fine, branching, and interconnected pseudopods that form a dynamic, net-like structure.

They are used for trapping food, locomotion, and in the construction of their tests (shells).

Axopodia (Radiolaria): These are long, thin, needle-like pseudopods, each supported by a central rod of microtubules (the axoneme).

They radiate outwards from the cell body and are used for buoyancy, capturing food, and sensation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Retaria…
2. Production of tests or skeletons

Most members of Retaria produce a protective external shell, called a test, or an intricate internal skeleton.
Foraminifera: Typically construct tests made of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).

These tests can be single-chambered or, more commonly, multi-chambered, with new chambers added as the organism grows.
Their porous shells allow pseudopods to extend outward for feeding and buoyancy.

Radiolaria: Famous for their beautiful and complex internal skeletons, most commonly made of silica (SiO₂).

These glass-like structures often feature intricate radial symmetry and long spines that increase surface area to slow sinking.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
ii. Ciliates (e.g., Tintinnids)
Phylum Ciliophora
Characteristics

Characterized by the presence of hair-like organelles called cilia, used for both locomotion and creating feeding currents.
Tintinnids are a prominent group of planktonic ciliates that build protective, vase-shaped shells called loricae, which can be composed of secreted minerals or cemented environmental particles.
They are important grazers of nanoplankton (phytoplankton and bacteria), acting as a vital link in the microbial loop by making energy available to larger zooplankton.







Note


Ciliates are often the most abundant microzooplankton in both marine and freshwater environments, capable of rapid population growth.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Ciliophora

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
iii. Heterotrophic Dinoflagellates
Phylum Dinoflagellata
Characteristics

They typically have two flagella.
They are significant predators of smaller zooplankton and other protists.
While many dinoflagellates are photosynthetic (phytoplankton), heterotrophic species lack chloroplasts and must ingest food.
Some species are mixotrophic, meaning they can both photosynthesize and ingest prey.







Note


Heterotrophic dinoflagellates play a major role in the microbial loop by consuming bacteria and small phytoplankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
2. Metazoan Zooplankton

Metazoans are multicellular animals.
This group contains the vast majority of zooplankton diversity, spanning numerous animal phyla.
They range from microscopic rotifers to giant jellyfish and include the most abundant animals on Earth, the copepods.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Arthropoda
Subphylum Crustacea

This is often the most dominant group in terms of biomass and abundance, it includes the copepods group.
They possess chitinous exoskeleton which provides support and protection.
They have two pairs of antenna used for sensing the environment and sometimes for swimming or feeding.
Limbs are typically two-branched (Biramous appendages), though often modified for various functions like swimming, feeding, or gas exchange.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Subphylum Crustacea…

They possess mandibles – jaw-like appendages for biting and grinding food.
Compound eyes often present, may be stalked or sessile.
Many are Nauplius larval stage.
The body segment typically divided into a head, thorax, and abdomen, though fusion of segments (eg cephalothorax) is common.

Class Maxillopoda

Maxillopoda is a large class of mostly small crustaceans (typically 0.5–2mm)
It consists of over 14,000 species of freshwater and marine copepods

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Subclass Copepoda

They have small body size with a cylindrical or pear-shaped body divided into two main regions
They possess a single, simple naupliar eye in the adult stage (though some larval forms might have paired eyes).
Have prominent first antennae (antennules) usually long, often used for swimming, sensory perception, and in males, for grasping females during mating.
Unlike many other crustaceans, copepods generally lack a true carapace covering the thorax.

Class Branchiopoda, Order Cladocera

These are small crustaceans with a bivalved carapace that encloses the body but not the head.
They have large, branched antennae used for swimming.
They are found in marine waters.
Cladocerans eat phytoplankton and other zooplankton.
Like many species of zooplankton, cladocerans migrate to the surface at night.
This is referred to as diurnal migration.

Order Amphipoda

They have lateral compressed body, they are typically flattened from side to side.
Unlike many other malacostracans, amphipods lack a carapace.
Many species are transparent or translucent, providing camouflage in the open ocean environment.
They have sessile compound eyes; their eyes are directly attached to the head, not on stalks which cover almost the entire surface of their head.
This is an adaptation for vision in the dim light of the pelagic (open ocean) environments they inhabit.
Their gills are located on the basal segments (coxae) of their thoracic legs.

Order Decapoda

Includes the larval stages (e.g., nauplius, zoea, megalopa) of many familiar benthic or nektonic crustaceans like crabs and lobsters.
They are primarily meroplankton, meaning they spend only their early life stages drifting in the water column.
They possess ten legs (pereiopods); the name Decapoda literally translates to “ten-footed.”
They feature a well-developed carapace covering the cephalothorax, which protects the internal organs and encloses the gill chambers.

Phylum Cnidaria



Phylum Cnidaria contains colonial siphonophores and scyphozoans (true jellyfish).
They are primarily marine predators equipped with stinging tentacles.
They possess specialized stinging cells called nematocysts used for defense and capturing prey.
Phylum Ctenophora (comb jellies) are often grouped with jellyfish but lack nematocysts, moving instead via rows of fused cilia.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Defense Mechanisms

Cnidarians have evolved various defense mechanisms to protect themselves from predators and other threats in their environment. These mechanisms include:

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Defense Mechanisms…

Nematocysts: These are specialized stinging cells that contain a coiled, barbed thread. When triggered, the thread is rapidly ejected, delivering venom to the target. Nematocysts can be used for both defense and capturing prey.
Bioluminescence: Many cnidarians can produce light through a chemical reaction in their bodies. This bioluminescence can serve as a defense mechanism by startling predators or by attracting larger predators to attack the initial threat.
Camouflage: Some cnidarians can blend into their surroundings, making them less visible to predators. This can involve changing color or having a body shape that mimics the environment.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Defense Mechanisms…

Toxicity: In addition to the venom delivered by nematocysts, some cnidarians produce toxins that can deter predators. These toxins can cause pain, paralysis, or even death in some cases.
Physical structures: Some cnidarians have physical structures that provide protection, such as a hard exoskeleton or a tough outer layer that can make them less palatable or more difficult for predators to consume.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Defense Mechanisms…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Chordata, Subphylum Tunicata

They are unique chordates that often retain a tadpole-like larval form (notochord and dorsal nerve cord) throughout their life cycle.
Planktonic tunicates include the holoplanktonic classes Appendicularia (larvaceans) and Thaliacea (salps, doliolids, and pyrosomes).
They are highly efficient filter feeders; Appendicularia creates a complex “mucous house” to trap ultra-fine food particles.
While some are holoplanktonic, many other tunicates (like sea squirts) are benthic as adults and only join the plankton as short-lived larvae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Chordata, Subphylum Vertebrata

They include fish Larvae (Ichthyoplankton)
They are eggs and larval stages of fish that drift passively with water currents.
Larvae often look significantly different from adult fish, lacking features like fully formed fins, scales, or pigmentation.
They are a form of meroplankton, as they only spend their early life stages in the plankton before developing into nektonic (swimming) adults.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Subphylum Vertebrata…

Many possess a fin fold instead of distinct fins.
Eyes are often relatively large and functional, for detecting food and predators.
They possess a lateral line system for detecting water movement and olfactory organs for smell.
Pharyngeal slits are modified into functional gills supported by gill arches.
They are highly vulnerable to predation and environmental changes, making them important indicators for fisheries management.
Their survival is closely linked to the timing of phytoplankton and zooplankton blooms (the “match-mismatch” hypothesis).

Phylum Chaetognatha (Arrow Worms)

They are commonly known as arrow worms – They are voracious marine predators.
They have transparent, torpedo-shaped body which is tapered at both ends, providing a streamlined form for rapid, darting movements.
They have a distinct body regions which is clearly divided into a head, trunk, and tail.
Posses a grasping spines: The head features formidable chitinous spines used to seize and hold prey (primarily copepods).
They possess one or two pairs of lateral fins for stabilization and a powerful caudal (tail) fin for propulsion.
They are hermaphroditic, possessing both male and female reproductive organs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Mollusca

Includes both meroplanktonic larvae of benthic mollusks (like veligers) and holoplanktonic specialists like pteropods (sea butterflies) and heteropods.
They possess a soft, unsegmented body, often with a muscular “foot” modified into wing-like lobes (parapodia) for swimming.
Some species (e.g., Atlanta) maintain a small, coiled, aragonitic shell for protection.
Others (e.g., Carinaria) have a reduced, cap-like shell or have lost the shell entirely to increase buoyancy and mobility.
Pteropods are highly sensitive to ocean acidification, as their thin calcium carbonate shells dissolve easily in lower pH waters.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phylum Annelida, Class Polychaeta

Polychaeta means “many bristles,” referring to the numerous chitinous bristles called chaetae arranged in bundles.
Each body segment typically features a pair of fleshy, two-branched (biramous) protrusions called parapodia used for locomotion and respiration.
They possess a well-developed head region, often equipped with sophisticated sensory organs including tentacles, palps, and eyespots.
While many are benthic (like the lugworm Arenicola marina), many species are holoplanktonic or have planktonic larval stages.
Planktonic forms are often transparent and use their parapodia as paddles for active swimming.
They play a significant role in the marine food web as both predators and prey for larger invertebrates and fish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton



The word plankton comes from a Greek word planktos, meaning drifter or wanderer.
Aquatic organisms that are incapable of swimming or moving on their own.
Their movement in water is aided by water movement such as tides, currents, waves
Most plankton are microscopic, often less than one inch in length.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton…



They also include larger species like some crustaceans and jellyfish.
Some organisms are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Others are planktonic only when they are young, but they eventually grow large enough to swim against the currents.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of Plankton

Plankton can be classified in several ways based on their;

Size
Life cycle
Trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on size

Based on their size, plankton can be classified as

Megaplankton – > 20 cm eg. Jellyfish
Macroplankton – 2-20 cm eg. Pteropods, krill
Mesoplankton – 0.2 mm-2 cm eg. copepods, Foraminifera
Microplankton – 20-200 \({\mu}m\) eg. Ciliates, coccolithophores
Nanoplankton – 2-20 \({\mu}m\) eg. Diatoms, dinoflagellates
Picoplankton – 0.2-2 \({\mu}m\) eg. smaller eukaryotic protists, bacteria
Femtoplankton – < 0.2 \({\mu}m\) eg. marine viruses

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Depending on mode of life cycle, plankton can be classified as:

Holoplankton or
Meroplankton

Holoplankton are organisms that are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Holoplankton lives in pelagic zone in aquatic environment

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Pelagic organisms are divided into two groups; plankton and nekton.
Nektons are capable of swimming against water current and include most species of sharks, rays, fishes, seals, sea lions, and whales.
Examples of holoplankton are some diatoms, radiolarians, some dinoflagellates, foraminifera, amphipods, krill, copepods, and salps, as well as some gastropod mollusk species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Meroplankton are aquatic organisms which have both planktonic and benthic stages in their life cycles.
Many of the meroplankton consists of larval stages of larger organism (Stübner et al., 2016)
After a period of time in the plankton, many meroplankton changes to the nekton or adopt a benthic (often sessile) lifestyle on the seafloor.
Many benthic community are planktonic during their larval stages (Ershova et al., 2019)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

The planktonic larval stage is particularly crucial to many benthic invertebrate in order to disperse their young.
Depending on particular species and environmental conditions, larval or juvenile-stage meroplankton may remain in the pelagic zone for duration ranging from an hour to months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on Trophic level

Plankton can also be divided based on the trophic level
This is the most basic category that divide plankton into two groups;

Phytoplankton – plants and
Zooplankton – animals.

Zooplankton and other small marine organisms feed on phytoplankton and they become food for fish, crustaceans, and other larger species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distribution of plankton

Plankton inhabits oceans, seas, lakes and ponds.
Their abundance varies both horizontally, vertically and seasonally.
This is due to availability of light.
Another factor causing plankton variation is nutrient availability.
Although large areas of tropical and sub-tropical oceans have abundant light, but they experience relatively low primary production because they offer limited nutrients such as nitrate, phosphate, iron and silicate.
In such regions, primary production usually occurs at greater depth, but at a reduced level due to limited nutrient level.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Aquatic environment divisions

Aquatic ecosystems can be sub-divided into:

Vertical division
Horizontal division

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical division

This has two sub-groups:

Benthic

Entire bottom, or the substratum and its attached and crawling organisms on the substratum.

Pelagic

Entire water body excluding the benthic system; It deals with floating and swimming organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal division

Two major parts:

Neritic province: – Shallow water above the continental shelf.
Oceanic: – Deep water beyond the continental shelf.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies used in Plankton Ecology
Plankton systematics

The study of the classification and taxonomy of planktonic organisms.
It involves identifying and categorizing different species of plankton based on their morphological, physiological, ecological, and genetic characteristics of planktonic organisms.
This field helps us understand the diversity and evolutionary relationships among planktonic species, as well as their distribution patterns and ecological roles in aquatic ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton ecology

Plankton ecology is the study of planktonic organisms and their interactions with each other and their environment within aquatic ecosystems.
It includes the distribution, abundance, diversity, behavior, physiology, and ecological roles of plankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton productivity

Plankton productivity refers to the rate at which planktonic organisms produce organic matter through photosynthesis and other metabolic processes.
It is a critical component of aquatic ecosystems, as plankton are the primary producers that form the base of the food web and support the entire ecosystem.
Understanding plankton productivity is essential for understanding the dynamics of aquatic ecosystems and the impacts of human activities on these ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton?

Phytoplankton is derived from the Greek words:

Phyto – (plant)
Plankton — (wanderer or drifter)

Phytoplankton are microscopic aquatic plants which do not have ability to swim
They are free-floating aquatic organisms that depend entirely on water movement such as surface currents for their movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton…

They thrive in euphotic zone; the upper water column of the world’s oceans, lakes, rivers, ponds.
The euphotic zone receives energy from the sun that allows phytoplankton to photosynthesize – a chemical reaction that converts light energy into chemical energy (Peter et al., 2018).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Phytoplankton…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton Ecology?

Definition: Study of phytoplankton organisms and their interactions with each other and their aquatic environment
Key focus areas:

Distribution and abundance patterns
Physiological responses to environmental factors
Population dynamics and growth rates
Ecological roles in food webs and nutrient cycling
Community structure and biodiversity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology



Phytoplankton contain Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) – a green pigment responsible for photosynthesis
Chl-a captures light energy and converts it into chemical energy (glucose)
This is the foundation of all aquatic food webs


Key products:

Organic matter (glucose) - food for consumers
Oxygen (O₂) - byproduct for respiration
Accounts for ~50% of Earth’s oxygen production

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis…
The Photosynthetic Equation:
\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\]

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Productivity

Definition: Rate at which phytoplankton produce organic matter through photosynthesis
Two types:

Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total organic matter produced
Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Organic matter remaining after phytoplankton respiration

Global significance: Phytoplankton account for ~50% of total organic matter production on Earth

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Controlling Phytoplankton Growth
1. Light (Photosynthetically Active Radiation - PAR)

PAR is the portion of sunlight (400-700 nm) that phytoplankton can use for photosynthesis
Light is required for photosynthesis
It penetrates only upper 200 meters (euphotic zone)
Its intensity decreases with depth
Affects vertical distribution of phytoplankton
Limiting factor: In deep water and winter months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
2. Nutrient Availability


Macronutrients:

Nitrogen (N) - essential for proteins and nucleic acids
Phosphorus (P) - crucial for ATP and DNA production
Silica (Si) - needed by diatoms for cell walls
Carbon (C) - primary building block of photosynthesis


Micronutrients/Trace Elements:

Iron (Fe) - critical enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Manganese (Mn) - photosynthesis component
Zinc (Zn) - enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Copper (Cu) - respiration and photosynthesis component



Nutrient limitation: Occurs when essential nutrients become depleted
Some phytoplankton can fix nitrogen and can grow in areas where nitrate concentrations are low.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
3. Temperature

Affects metabolic rates and enzyme activity in phytoplankton
Optimal range: 15-30°C for most species, in tropics, optimal temperature is between 25-30°C
Seasonal effects: Growth varies with seasonal temperature changes
Geographic pattern: Higher productivity in temperate zones seasonally, but lower in tropical areas

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
4. Salinity

Determines osmotic balance for phytoplankton cells
Influences nutrient availability and uptake
Affects species composition (freshwater vs. marine species)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
5. Water Turbulence and Mixing

Vertical mixing: Brings nutrient-rich deeper water to surface
Prevents sinking: Keeps phytoplankton in euphotic zone
Optimal conditions: Moderate mixing

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
6. Grazing Pressure

Predation by zooplankton: Controls phytoplankton biomass
Top-down control: High grazing = lower phytoplankton abundance
Species selectivity: Zooplankton prefer certain phytoplankton types

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutrient Limitation and the “Redfield Ratio”

Redfield Ratio: C:N:P = 106:16:1
Represents the average elemental composition of phytoplankton biomass
Used to predict which nutrient will limit growth

Application:

If actual N:P ratio < 16:1 → Nitrogen limited
If actual N:P ratio > 16:1 → Phosphorus limited
Helps predict ecosystem productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton Growth Phases: Overview

Phytoplankton populations follow a characteristic growth pattern with four distinct phases
Each phase represents different population dynamics
Understanding these phases is crucial for predicting bloom events and managing aquatic ecosystems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 1: Lag Phase (Adaptation)


Characteristics:

Phytoplankton acclimate to new environment
Low growth rate
Enzyme and nutrient uptake mechanisms are activated
Cells preparing for rapid growth

Duration:

Variable, typically days to weeks
Depends on environmental conditions


What is happening:

Cells synthesize enzymes
Photosynthetic machinery activated
Nutrient transport systems established
Building internal reserves

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 2: Exponential (Log) Growth Phase


Characteristics:

Rapid population growth under optimal conditions
Resources are abundant
Biomass doubles at regular intervals
Maximum growth rate achieved

Duration:

Weeks to months
Continues while resources available


What is happening:

Cells dividing at maximum rate
Nutrients abundant
Light optimal
Waste products not yet limiting
This is when algal blooms occur

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 3: Stationary Phase


Characteristics:

Growth rate equals death rate
Nutrient depletion begins
Waste accumulation occurs
Biomass plateaus at carrying capacity

Duration:

Variable, typically weeks
Reflects resource availability


What is happening:

Nutrient availability declining
Competition for resources intensifying
Toxin accumulation possible
Population stabilizes at maximum sustainable level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 4: Death (Decline) Phase


Characteristics:

Nutrients are exhausted
Toxic metabolites accumulate
Death rate exceeds birth rate
Population crashes

Duration:

Days to weeks
Depends on toxin levels


What is happening:

Essential nutrients depleted
Waste products toxic to cells
Cell lysis and death accelerates
Biomass rapidly decreases
Dead cells sink (detritus)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Primary Production:


Phytoplankton are primary producers, meaning they convert sunlight, carbon dioxide, and nutrients into organic matter through photosynthesis
They form the base of the marine food web, providing energy and nutrients to higher trophic levels.
Phytoplankton are the foundation of all aquatic food web

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration:

Phytoplankton absorb CO₂ from the atmosphere during photosynthesis and incorporate it into their biomass.
Through photosynthesis, phytoplankton consume carbon dioxide on a scale equivalent to forests and other land plants.
Some of this carbon is carried to the deep ocean when phytoplankton die – effectively removing carbon from the atmosphere and contributing to the regulation of climate.
Some is transferred to different layers of the ocean as phytoplankton are eaten by other creatures, which themselves reproduce, generate waste, and die.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration…:

Worldwide, this biological carbon pump transfers about 10 gigatonnes of carbon from the atmosphere to the deep ocean each year.
Even a small changes in the growth of phytoplankton may affect atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations, which would feed back to global surface temperatures.
How ????

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Carbon flux in the ocean

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
What is the Biological Carbon Pump?

Mechanism by which carbon dioxide (CO₂) is converted into organic matter by phytoplankton and transported to the deep ocean
Acts as a major regulator of atmospheric CO₂ and global climate

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
The Process (Four key steps):

Photosynthetic Fixation (Surface)

Phytoplankton fix atmospheric CO₂ into organic compounds (glucose, proteins, lipids)
Occurs in euphotic zone (0-200 m) where light is available

Food Web Transfer (Surface to Mid-Water)

Zooplankton consume phytoplankton
Fish consume zooplankton
Energy and carbon transfer up the food chain
~90% of energy lost at each trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…

Particle Sinking (Active Transport)

Dead phytoplankton cells form aggregates (“marine snow”)
Dead zooplankton and fecal pellets sink
Particles fall through water column (1-100 m/day)
Passive transport of carbon to depth

Remineralization (Deep Ocean)

Bacteria decompose sinking organic matter
Some carbon sequestered in deep ocean (1000+ years residence time)
Some carbon recycled back to surface via upwelling

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
Global Carbon Flux Numbers:

~10 gigatonnes of carbon/year transferred from atmosphere to deep ocean
~50% of Earth’s primary production is from phytoplankton
~0.5-2 gigatonnes/year permanently sequestered in deep ocean
Equivalent to all carbon emissions from fossil fuels and deforestation combined!

Why This Matters for Climate:

Reduces atmospheric CO₂ by ~25-50% compared to no biological pump
Small changes in phytoplankton productivity = major climate impacts
Ocean warming/acidification affects phytoplankton → reduced CO₂ removal → positive feedback loop

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Food Source:


Phytoplankton are consumed by a wide range of marine organisms, including zooplankton, small fish, shellfish, and whales.
They feed all aquatic organisms from microscopic, animal-like zooplankton to multi-ton whales.
Some small fish and invertebrates they also graze on the phytoplankton, and then those smaller animals are eaten by bigger ones.
They are essential food sources for many marine species, forming the foundation of marine food webs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nitrogen fixation


Nitrogen fixation is the biological process by which certain microorganisms, primarily bacteria and cyanobacteria, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other nitrogen compounds that can be used by plants and other organisms.
They contribute significant amounts of fixed nitrogen to marine ecosystems.
This input of fixed nitrogen can alleviate nitrogen limitation in phytoplankton communities, promoting their growth and productivity.
Example of nitrogen fixers are such as Trichodesmium and Richelia species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nutrient Cycling:


Phytoplankton contribute to nutrient cycling in marine ecosystems by recycling nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and iron.
They play a vital role in maintaining nutrient balance and productivity in marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Indicator of Ecosystem Health:


Changes in phytoplankton populations can serve as indicators of environmental changes and ecosystem health.
Monitoring phytoplankton abundance, species composition, and distribution can provide insights into water quality, nutrient levels, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Climate Regulation:


Phytoplankton influence climate by reducing atmospheric CO2 levels during photosynthesis process

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Oxygen Production:


Phytoplankton produce a significant portion of oxygen on the Earth through photosynthesis, contributing more than 50% of the oxygen in the Earth’s atmosphere.
They play a major role in maintaining global oxygen levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Phytoplankton

Biotechnological Applications:


Some species of phytoplankton are used in biotechnological applications, such as producing biofuels, pharmaceuticals, and nutritional supplements.
For example, certain types of microalgae are cultivated for their high lipid content, which can be converted into biodiesel.
Microalgae with the highest lipid content, ranging from 10 to 67%, belong to Chlorella, Dunaliella, and Scenedesmus species
A Spirulina species – a blue-green algae, holds immense potential in the field of biotechnology.
Its ability to produce valuable secondary metabolites like nutraceuticals and pharmaceuticals makes it a promising source for drug development
They are effective in managing allergies, cancer, and other diseases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
10. Aquaculture

Phytoplankton are a key food item in both aquaculture and mariculture.
Both utilize phytoplankton as food for the animals being farmed.
In mariculture, the phytoplankton is naturally occurring and is introduced into enclosures with the normal circulation of seawater.
In aquaculture, phytoplankton must be introduced directly.
The plankton can either be collected from a body of water or cultured.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
11. Economic Importance of Phytoplankton

Phytoplanktons have several uses and their mass cultivation can also be a major source of income.
They are mostly used as food supplements and play a vital role in both animal and human nutrition.
Phytoplanktons are also used in the manufacture of drugs.
Also, they have proven to be suitable for synthesizing vaccines.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:

PhytoplanktonApplicationChlorella vulgarisFood supplement, animal feedSpirulinaFood supplement and cosmeticsOdontella auritaCosmetics, pharmaceuticals and food for both adults and infantsPhaeodactylum tricornutumFuel production and nutritionPorphyridium cruentumCosmetics, pharmaceuticals and nutrition

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
12. Negative importance of phytoplankton

Some phytoplankton species can be the source of death or disease to other aquatic organisms.
Certain species of phytoplankton produce powerful biotoxins – leading to the development of red tides, or harmful algal blooms (HABs).
These toxic blooms can kill marine life and people who eat contaminated seafood.
Phytoplankton may cause mass mortality in other ways.
In the aftermath of a massive bloom, dead phytoplankton sink to the ocean or lake floor.
Then, decomposition occurs aided by bacteria.
This process lead to depletion of oxygen in the water, causing to suffocation of animals.
This may result to a massive death of aquatic organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Bacillariophyta (Diatoms)

Bacillariophyta, commonly known as diatoms, are a major group of phytoplankton distinguished by their silica-based cell walls (frustules).
They inhabit diverse aquatic ecosystems including freshwater, brackish, and marine waters, as well as wetlands and moist soils.
Diatoms are ecologically critical, comprising a significant portion of marine phytoplankton and contributing up to 20–50% of global photosynthetic carbon fixation.
They exhibit diverse lifestyles: some are benthic (attached to substrates), while others are planktonic, and many possess a raphe—a specialized slit-like structure that enables active gliding motility over sediment surfaces.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics features of diatoms
1. Symmetry and Morphology



Diatoms are divided into two major groups based on symmetry:

Pennate diatoms
Centric diatoms

Pennate diatoms: Bilaterally symmetric (elongated, boat-shaped, or pen/needle-like).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Centric diatoms: Radially symmetric (circular, triangular, or cylindrical).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
2. Silica-based cell wall (frustule)



A cell wall of diatoms are characterised by having an external skeleton called frustule made of hydrated silica (\(SiO_2.nH_2O\)), which is like a box.
The lid of the box is called a valve and the sides are made of a series of bands called girdle.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The frustule composed of a larger half called the epitheca fitting over a separate, slightly smaller half known as a hypotheca.

An epitheca – larger upper valve and
A hypotheca – smaller lower valve that fit together like a petri dish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The valve & bands are not solid silica, but have open structures
An individual opening is called areola
The areolae may be arranged in rows called striae

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: In centric diatoms the striae are oriented towards a point and in pennate diatoms towards a line.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
3. Locomotion

Pennate diatoms have a raphe, a slit-like structure that secretes mucopolysaccharides, allowing them to glide on surfaces.
Centric diatoms lack a raphe and are mostly non-motile, relying on water currents for movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
4. Pigments and photosynthesis

They possess chlorophyll-a & c (no chlorophyll b).
They also have fucoxanthin (a brown carotenoid) gives diatoms their golden-brown color.
They store energy as chrysolaminarin instead of starch.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
5. Reproduction

A dominant mode of reproduction is asexual reproduction by binary fission, where each daughter cell inherits one half of the frustule and regenerates the smaller half.
Over time, this leads to size reduction.
They also undergo sexual reproduction (size restoration):

Pennate diatoms: Auxospore formation (fusion of gametes to restore original size).
Centric diatoms: Oogamy (large non-motile egg + small motile sperm).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of diatoms

The phylum Bacillariophyta (diatoms) is traditionally divided into two main classes, primarily based on symmetry and morphological features of their silica frustules;


Class bacillariophyceae (pennate diatoms)
Class coscinodiscophyceae (centric diatoms)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
1. Class bacillariophyceae (pennate diatoms)

Body symmetry: – Bilateral (elongated, boat-shaped, or needle-like).
Many possess a raphe (slit for gliding motility).
Mostly benthic (attached to surfaces), but some planktonic.
Reproduction: Auxospores restore cell size after division.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology

They are bilaterally symmetric (elongated, boat-shaped

text:
ii). Order bacillariales



Order Bacillariales is a group of raphid pennate diatoms known for their elongated, needle-like or sigmoid (S-shaped) frustules and active motility.
They are found in both freshwater and marine environments.
Examples are Nitzschia, Hantzschia

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology

Morphology



They have an elongated, needle-like, or sigmoid (S-shaped) shape.
Some have keeled raphe systems (Nitzschia).
Unlike the central raphe of Naviculales, the keeled raphe is elevated on a raised ridge or keel along the edge of the valve
They have fine parallel lines (striae) or (pores) on the valve surface.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Motility

Raphe secretes mucopolysaccharides, enabling active gliding on surfaces.

This helps in escaping sedimentation, nutrient foraging, and biofilm formation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
General characteristics
Taxonomy & diversity:

Approximately 2,000-4,000 species described worldwide.
Primarily marine (90%), but also found in freshwater and some terrestrial habitats.
Both photosynthetic (autotrophic) and heterotrophic species exist in this group.
Size ranges between 20-200 μm, though some species reach 2 mm (Noctiluca).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distinctive morphological features of dinoflagellates


1. Flagellar apparatus:

Possess two flagella with distinct orientations:

Transverse flagellum: Lies in the cingulum (groove encircling the cell), creates spinning motion.
Longitudinal flagellum: Extends posteriorly from the sulcus (longitudinal groove), provides forward propulsion.





This arrangement produces the characteristic spinning, tumbling motion (dinos = whirling).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distinctive morphological features…


2. Cell covering:

Thecate (armored): Some possess cellulose plates called theca/thecae arranged in a protective lorica.

Plates organized in specific patterns (tabulation).
Provides structural support and protection.

Some are Athecate (naked): Lack rigid plates, have only a cell membrane.

More flexible but less protected.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distinctive morphological features…
3. Unique dinokaryon nucleus:

Chromosomes remain permanently condensed throughout cell cycle.
DNA not associated with histones (uses different proteins).
Nuclear envelope persists during division (closed mitosis).
This unique nuclear structure distinguishes dinoflagellates from other eukaryotes.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Pigmentation & photosynthesis in dinoflagellates
Autotrophic species:

Chloroplasts contain:

Chlorophyll a and c.
Peridinin (unique carotenoid giving golden-brown color).
Some species have different pigments due to secondary endosymbiosis.

Autotrophs perform photosynthesis, contributing to primary production.

Storage products:

They store energy as starch and lipids.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutritional Modes of dinoflagellates
1. Autotrophic:

Some are photosynthetic (e.g., Ceratium, Peridinium).

2. Heterotrophic:

Some are heterotrophic (predators or parasites, e.g., Pfiesteria).
They ingest bacteria, other protists, or organic particles.
Some are parasitic (e.g., Pfiesteria).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutritional Modes…
3. Mixotrophic:

Some are Mixotrophs (both photosynthetic and heterotrophic).
Combine photosynthesis with prey ingestion.
Provides nutritional flexibility (e.g., Dinophysis, Karlodinium).

4. Symbiotic:

Zooxanthellae (Symbiodinium): Live symbiotically in coral tissues, providing photosynthates.
Critical for coral reef health and survival.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Reproduction in dinoflagellates
Asexual reproduction (dominant):

Asexual reproduction is by Binary fission – cell divides longitudinally.
This leads to rapid population growth during favorable conditions.

Sexual reproduction:

This occurs less frequently.
It involves gamete fusion and meiosis.
It is often triggered by environmental stress.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Reproduction in dinoflagellates…
Resting cysts:

Many species form resting cysts (hypnozygotes):

This is the thick-walled, dormant stages.
Normally sink to sediments.
It germinate when conditions improve.
This is important for surviving adverse conditions and dispersal.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological roles of dinoflagellates
1. Primary production:

Support food webs in coastal and oceanic environments.
Major contributors to marine phytoplankton biomass.

2. Coral symbiosis:

Zooxanthellae provide up to 90% of coral energy requirements.
Coral bleaching occurs when symbionts are expelled.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological roles of dinoflagellates…
3. Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) – Red Tides

Higher nutrient enrichment can lead to rapid population growth of certain species of dinoflagellates in aquatic ecosystems (e.g., Karenia, Alexandrium).
The growth can cause water discoloration (reddish-brown).
These species also produce potent toxins that can accumulate in shellfish and fish, posing risks to human health and marine life.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Harmful Algal Blooms…

The toxins produced include:

Saxitoxins (Alexandrium sp) → Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP).
Brevetoxins (Karenia sp) → Neurotoxic Shellfish Poisoning (NSP).
Okadaic acid (Dinophysis sp, Prorocentrum sp) → Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP).
Ciguatoxins (Gambierdiscus sp) → Ciguatera Fish Poisoning.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological roles of dinoflagellates…

The toxins can cause various illnesses in humans, such as paralytic shellfish poisoning (PSP), neurotoxic shellfish poisoning (NSP), and diarrhetic shellfish poisoning (DSP).
They can also lead to fish kills and marine mammal deaths, resulting in significant ecological and economic impacts on fisheries and tourism industries.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological roles of dinoflagellates…
4. Bioluminescence

Some species of dinoflagellates emit light when disturbed (e.g., Noctiluca sp, Lingulodinium sp).
These produce spectacular glowing waves at night.
They uses luciferin-luciferase reaction.
This may serve as defense mechanism against predators.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Taxonomy of Dinoflagellates
Class Dinophyceae

This is the largest and most diverse class of dinoflagellates, containing the majority of known species.
It includes both photosynthetic and heterotrophic species, as well as many that are mixotrophic or symbiotic.
Members of this class are found in a wide range of aquatic environments, from freshwater to marine habitats.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Major Orders of Dinophyceae

This class is divided into several orders based on morphological and genetic characteristics. The major orders include:


Gonyaulacales (e.g., Alexandrium — toxin producers).
Peridiniales (e.g., Peridinium).
Prorocentrales (e.g., Prorocentrum).
Dinophysiales (e.g., Dinophysis — diarrhetic shellfish poisoning).
Noctilucales (non-photosynthetic, bioluminescent).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Gonyaulacales

Composed of a theca – thecate with cellulose plates arranged in specific tabulation patterns.
Often have a polygonal or spherical shape, with pronounced cingulum (transverse groove).
Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll a, c, and peridinin.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Gonyaulacales…


Some species produce Toxin;

Alexandrium (saxitoxins → paralytic shellfish poisoning/PSP).
Gonyaulax (yessotoxins → diarrhetic shellfish poisoning/DSP).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Gonyaulacales…


Bloom formation:

Lingulodinium (bioluminescent; forms red tides).
Ceratocorys (tropical, armored species).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Gonyaulacales…
Bioluminescence

Some species (e.g., Lingulodinium polyedra) emit light via luciferase enzymes.
Most are free-living, rarely symbiosis

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Peridiniales



Composed of armored (thecate) with cellulose plates arranged in a distinctive tabulation pattern.
Typically compressed anteroposteriorly, with a pronounced cingulum (transverse groove) and sulcus (longitudinal groove).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Peridiniales…

Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll a, c₂, and accessory pigments like peridinin (in autotrophic species).
Many species are mixotrophic or fully heterotrophic (e.g., Protoperidinium).
Some species produce toxins.
Many species produce resting cysts.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Peridiniales…
Key genera:

Peridinium (freshwater/marine; often bloom-forming).
Scrippsiella (calcareous cysts; common in coastal waters).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Prorocentrales



The Prorocentrales are a small order of dinoflagellates.
They are laterally compressed, often heart-shaped or oval.
They are distinguished by having their two flagella inserted apically, rather than ventrally as in other groups.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Prorocentrales…



One flagellum extends forward and the other circles its base, and there are no flagellar grooves.
This arrangement is called desmokont, in contrast to the dinokont arrangement found in other groups.
Accordingly, the Prorocentrales may be called desmoflagellates, and in some classifications were treated as a separate class Desmophyceae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Prorocentrales…

All members have chloroplasts and a theca, which is composed of two large plates joined by a sagittal suture.
This structure is shared with the Dinophysiales, and they are probably sister groups.
Several species produce toxins and form harmful algal blooms:

Prorocentrum lima: Produces okadaic acid (causes diarrhetic shellfish poisoning)
Prorocentrum cordatum: Common bloom-former in coastal waters
Prorocentrum minimum: Forms red tides, may be toxic

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Prorocentrales…
Distinctive features:

No cingulum or sulcus (unlike most dinoflagellates).
Two large symmetrical thecal plates.
Apical pore complex where flagella emerge.
Example:

Prorocentrum (most diverse genus, includes toxic species).
Mesoporos (less common, marine species).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Dinophysiales



Laterally compressed cells with prominent wing-like lists (membranous extensions).
Theca structure: Thin cellulose plates with characteristic ridges and pores
Locomotion: Slow swimmers with reduced flagellar apparatus
They are mixotrophic – combination of autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Dinophysiales…



Several species produce toxins and causes diarrhetic shellfish poisoning (DSP)
Example:

Dinophysis (most significant genus, contains many toxic species)
Phalacroma (closely related, sometimes merged with Dinophysis)
Ornithocercus (notable for extremely developed lists)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Noctilucales



Unarmored cells: Lack cellulose plates (naked).
Size: Exceptionally large for dinoflagellates (200-2000 μm).
Bioluminescence: Most species produce light (luciferin-luciferase system).
They are exclusively heterotrophic.
They are exclusively heterotrophic, feeding on other plankton and organic matter.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Noctilucales



Some species have tentacle-like structures for prey capture
Vacuolated cytoplasm - contains large fluid-filled spaces
Have highly flexible cell membrane that allows shape changes during feeding
Example: Noctiluca scintillans (bioluminescent, forms red tides).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Overview

Harmful algal blooms (HABs) are high-density accumulations of algae that cause negative impacts on ecosystems, human health, and economies.
HABs are produced by diverse groups: dinoflagellates, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and others.

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Definitions

Bloom: a rapid increase in algal biomass in aquatic systems.
Harmful: when blooms produce toxins, cause hypoxia, clog fisheries, or otherwise disrupt ecosystem services.

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Major Causal Factors

Nutrient enrichment (nitrogen, phosphorus)
Water column stratification and stability
Elevated temperature and light
Hydrodynamic conditions (retention, upwelling)
Anthropogenic influences (runoff, sewage, aquaculture)

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Key Organisms

Dinoflagellates (e.g., Karenia, Alexandrium)
Diatoms (e.g., Pseudo-nitzschia)
Cyanobacteria (freshwater; e.g., Microcystis)
Others: raphidophytes, haptophytes

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Toxins and Human Health

Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) — saxitoxins
Diarrhetic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP) — okadaic acid
Amnesic Shellfish Poisoning (ASP) — domoic acid
Neurotoxic Shellfish Poisoning (NSP) — brevetoxins
Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins, cylindrospermopsin

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Ecological and Economic Impacts

Fish kills from hypoxia or toxins
Shellfish contamination and harvest closures
Tourism loss and water-use restrictions
Food-web alterations and biodiversity loss

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Monitoring & Detection

Remote sensing (satellite ocean colour)
In-situ sampling (net tows, water bottles)
Physiochemical sensors (fluorometers, CTD)
Molecular methods (qPCR, eDNA)
Toxin assays (LC-MS, ELISA)

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Management & Mitigation

Nutrient load reduction (catchment management)
Early warning systems and monitoring networks
Shellfish bed closures and public advisories
Physical removal and aeration (limited use)
Research into biological controls and forecasting

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Case Studies (examples)
1) Lake Erie — Microcystis bloom and Toledo (2014)

Event: Large Microcystis bloom contaminated drinking-water intake causing a short-term drinking water advisory for Toledo, Ohio.
Impacts: Municipal water supply disruption and heightened attention to watershed nutrient management.
Further reading: https://www.epa.gov/ground-water-and-drinking-water/toledo-water-crisis-2014

2) Gulf of Mexico — Karenia brevis (Florida red tides)

Event: Recurring Karenia brevis blooms periodically produce brevetoxins, causing fish kills, respiratory irritation, and shellfish bed closures.
Impacts: Economic losses to fisheries and tourism; human respiratory and shellfish-poisoning risks.
Monitoring: Florida Fish and Wildlife Research and NOAA maintain event reports and maps: https://myfwc.com/research/redtide/ and https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/

3) West Coast Pseudo-nitzschia — Domoic acid events (e.g., mid-2010s)

Event: Intense Pseudo-nitzschia blooms produced elevated domoic acid levels leading to shellfish fisheries closures, marine mammal and seabird strandings.
Impacts: Fisheries closures, wildlife mortality, and increased monitoring and forecasting efforts.
Further reading: NOAA Harmful Algal Bloom resources: https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Local & Global Monitoring Links

NOAA Harmful Algal Blooms Program: https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/hab/
IOC-UNESCO Harmful Algae Programme: https://hab.ioc-unesco.org/

Replace or add your local monitoring pages here (state agency, university lab, or marine monitoring network).

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Prevention and Policy

Land-use practices to reduce runoff
Wastewater treatment upgrades
Cross-sector coordination (health, environment, fisheries)

title: Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)
text:
Summary

HABs arise from a mix of natural and human-driven factors.
Effective response combines monitoring, pollution control, and public communication.
